responseSet.title: Obituary: Sudhir Kakar
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary: Sudhir Kakar

Nilanjana Sanyal

Nilanjana Sanyal

sanyal_nilanjana2004@rediffmail.com

Nilanjana Sanyal  

Sudhir Kakar, a pioneering Indian psychoanalyst, novelist, and author renowned for his work in cultural psychology and the psychology of religion, passed away on 22 April 2024 at the age of 85. Born on 25 July 1938 in Nainital, Uttarakhand, Kakar’s early life was marked by his experiences in both India and Pakistan during the partition. His formative years included education at Modern School, New Delhi, and St. Edward’s School, Shimla, followed by studies in mechanical engineering at Gujarat University. Kakar furthered his education in Europe, earning a master’s equivalent in business administration from the University of Mannheim and a doctorate in economics from the University of Vienna.

Kakar’s journey into psychoanalysis began at the University of Frankfurt’s Sigmund Freud Institute in 1971. Upon returning to India in 1975, he established a psychoanalytic practice in Delhi and briefly headed the Department of Humanities and Social Sciences at the Indian Institute of Technology. His illustrious career included teaching positions at prestigious institutions such as Harvard, the University of Chicago, McGill, Melbourne, Hawaii, and Vienna, among others.

A resident of Goa since 2003, Kakar continued his practice in Benaulim while serving as a Visiting Professor at Goa University. His controversial stance on the death penalty for child rape sparked significant debate in 2018, highlighting his complex and sometimes contentious views on societal issues.

Kakar’s scholarly contributions include profound analyses of figures such as Swami Vivekananda, Mohandas Gandhi, and Ramakrishna, exploring the interplay between psychoanalysis and mysticism. His novel Ecstasy delved into the spiritual landscape of India, capturing the essence of its mysticism. Despite critiques from peers like Alan Roland, Kakar’s work remains influential in understanding the psychological dimensions of spirituality.

His personal spirituality was a blend of profound connections with people, nature, art, and music, shaped by the contrasting influences of his rationalistic father and religious mother.

[[RunningHead]]

Finding Winnicott – Philosophical Encounters with the Psychoanalytic, by Fadi Abou-Rihan, Oxon and New York, Routledge, 2023, 174 pp., £29.99 (paperback), ISBN: 9781032391335

Marie Lenormand

Marie Lenormand

marie.anne.lenormand@gmail.com

Marie Lenormand  

Finding Winnicott

Fadi Abou-Rihan’s Finding Winnicott – Philosophical Encounters with the Psychoanalytic is composed of six chapters plus an introductory first chapter in which he presents his general aim and method. The chapters (two of which have been published previously) read more like independent essays, each tackling a specific aspect of his main project, that of “finding Winnicott” – a suggestive title that turns Winnicott’s paradoxical insights concerning how the infant “finds” the object onto Winnicott’s theory. As is well known, Winnicott writes in Playing and Reality that “the baby creates the object, but the object was there waiting to be created and to become a cathected object” (Winnicott 1971, 89). Somewhat cheekily, Abou-Rihan suggests that reading Winnicott requires following the exact same process. The main thrust of my review will be concerned with this fundamental aspect of his book.

Philosophical Encounters with the Psychoanalytic

This Winnicottian inspiration does not extend to Abou-Rihan’s use of language, however. In spite of his “particular use of the English vernacular”, as Jan Abram puts it (J. Abram, The Surviving Object: Psychoanalytic clinical essays on psychic survival-of-the-object (Routledge, 2021), xxiii), Winnicott’s language is famously characterized by its apparent simplicity and accessibility to non-specialists. Abou-Rihan, on the other hand, makes use of technical language and his perspective is distinctly philosophical, as evidenced for instance by the abstract chapter titles (Rationales – Findings – Series – Solitudes – Games – Spaces – Properties) or indeed the subtitle (Philosophical Encounters with the Psychoanalytic). In doing so, his aim is twofold. First, he intends to put Winnicott in dialogue with philosophy since, in his view, “less attention has been paid to the intersections between Winnicott’s thought and certain currents within the broader philosophical tradition” than between his work and “other figures in the [analytical] field” (2). Abou-Rihan is especially fond of Deleuze and Guattari’s writings, but also of “Benjamin, […] Huizinga, Ibn al-’Arabi, Laplanche, Bonaventure and Corbin” (11). I will return briefly later on to his use of philosophers.

[[RunningHead]]

On Freud's “Moses and Monotheism” edited by Lawrence J. Brown, Abington, Oxon (UK) and New York, Routledge, 2023, 196 pp., $39.95 (paperback), ISBN: 9781032223131

Robert Paul

Robert Paul

Atlanta, Georgia 30322, USA
rpaul@emory.edu

Robert Paul  

In the 1935 “Postscript” to his “Autobiographical Study” (1925), written while he was in the midst of composing “Moses and Monotheism”, Freud makes this remarkable assertion: “My interest, after making a lifelong detour through the natural sciences, medicine and psychotherapy, returned to the cultural problems which had fascinated me long before, when I was a youth scarcely old enough for thinking” (72). As for what those cultural problems were, he makes that clear in the opening pages of his “Autobiographical Study” itself: “My deep engrossment in the Bible story (almost as soon as I had learnt the art of reading) had, as I recognized much later, an enduring effect upon the direction of my interest” (8). Put those two things together and you get “Moses and Monotheism”. In that text (hereafter “Moses”), Freud applies the ontological developmental pattern of “trauma-repression-latency-return of the repressed”, derived during his “lifelong detour in psychotherapy” from his understanding of obsessional neurosis, to the analogous emergence of monotheistic religion at the group level. The original trauma in this case is the murder of Moses at the hands of his rebellious followers. Monotheism arose with compulsive force after a period of repression and latency as the defensive effect of the revived memory of the crime among those followers, who were to become the Jews.

In the book under review, nine authors, as well as the editor, address a range of different aspects of Freud’s often perplexing text from a diverse array of scholarly perspectives. (Due to the length of Freud’s text, the work itself is not included in this most recent entry into the book series “Contemporary Freud, Turning Points and Contemporary Issues”, as has been the case in past contributions to the series.) [[RunningHead]]

Letter to the Editor

Caron Harrang LICSW FIPA BCPsa , Drew Tillotson PsyD FIPA BCPsa and Nancy Winters MD FIPA ABPsa

Nancy Winters

cogster@earthlink.net
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-9459-1467

Nancy Winters  

To the Editor:

Recently, our book Body as Psychoanalytic Object: Clinical Applications from Winnicott to Bion and Beyond (Routledge, 2021) was reviewed by Michael Robbins (IJP, 103(6): 1133–1136). This review contains certain errors which we would like to correct. We feel some of his assertions were based on a broad misunderstanding of the focus of the book and we would like to clarify this.

Robbins asserts in several places his view that the book focuses on a particular type of patient “whose language does not express reflective thought but is dissociated from their basic experience and expression, which is somatic, affective and behavioral” (1133). He asserts again, “I return to this issue in my conclusion as it is at the core of working with unintegrated, dissociated persons, and is the focus of this book” (1133), and comments on Ricard and Jarreau’s chapter, “However, the chapter seemed unrelated to the general thesis of the book about working with dissociated individuals” (1134). He also states that most of the patients described are psychotic: “The undifferentiated, unintegrated, non-reflective somatic-behavioral mode that characterizes most of the patients described in the book is compatible with my understanding of psychosis” (1133). Alongside his homogeneous view of the patients described he also thinks the interventions in the book are homogeneous: “Most of the vignettes are about how the authors attempt with more or less success to meet the patient in mirror-like resonance at their functioning somatic-affective level and thereby establish an ‘I am with you’ sense of caring” (1136).

We feel Robbins’ understanding that the focus of the book is on a particular type of patient is not a legitimate reading of the book; it is a particularly odd reading in light of the fact that, as indicated in the book’s title, and as expressed in the Introduction and the thirteen diverse and separately authored chapters, the focus of the book is not on a particular type of patient but on the body as psychoanalytic object, in the Bionian sense. As described by Bion (1962, p. 68), “it is with the detection and observation of these [psychoanalytic] objects that the psychoanalyst must concern himself in the conduct of an analysis”. Such objects, like Bion’s selected fact, evolve in the shared emotional experience of the session. As we say in the Introduction to the book, each chapter is a kind of “psychoanalytic session” in which the body makes its appearance; each vignette presents the body as a unique type of psychoanalytic object for our analytic attention.

Gregorio Hautmann

Gregorio Hautmann

Firenze, Italia
gregorio.hautmann@gmail.com

Gregorio Hautmann  

If, as we know, the essence of trauma, setting aside its characteristics as a phenomenon, consists in not having found thoughts or words to represent it: if, that is, the path for working through it is by definition closed off, repetition is all that is left for expressing the unconscious of those who have suffered a trauma. This is a failure of, and at the same time an attempt at, symbolisation.

On the one hand it is a failure of symbolisation because, however many different guises repetition may adopt in each of us - for the most part these are actions, but sometimes they are silences instead, or words, ideas too, even theories, or feelings, sometimes passions, as well as dreams, naturally - nevertheless repetition invariably maintains the quality of an act. And, as such, it just as invariably induces a complete arrest of the passing of time. We might say it *Review translated by Adam Elgar.438

Thank You to Contributors and Reviewers for 2024

The Editors, Francis Grier, Lesley Steyn, Bruce Reis, Bernard Reith, Alfonso Pola, Georg Bruns, Sara Boffito, and Catherine Humble would like to express their gratitude to the team of reviewers throughout the world responsible for the successful implementation of the Journal’s peer-review process. These colleagues devote a great deal of time and effort in the service of providing detailed and thoughtful evaluations of manuscripts, film essays and book reviews which will ultimately help the authors strengthen their contributions. We would also like to thank the Section Editors for all their hard work: Heinz Weiss (Education Section), Elizabeth Allison (Psychoanalytic Controversies), Abbot Bronstein and Cynthia Ellis Gray (Analyst at Work), Georg Bruns (Psychoanalysis in the Community), Bruce Eadie (Film Essays), Sara Collins (Book Reviews), Gretchen Schmutz (North American Book Reviews), and all our Book Review Associates. We would also like to thank their Editorial teams. Without the dedication of the Section Editors and their teams, along with the reviewers, the IJP would not have achieved the scholarly status it enjoys. In addition, we would like to thank the Editor-in-Chief of the IJP Annuals, Melis Tanik Sivri, and all the IJP Annuals Editors and their Board Members who produce collections of IJP papers in various languages. We wish to express our appreciation to our regular translators, and to Jake Roberts for his important outreach work. Last but not least, we would like to thank Chris Lawrence, our Journal Assistant, for his ongoing hard work behind the scenes. We thank the following reviewers:

responseSet.title: (Re)translating Freud: Some Fundamental Questions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

(Re)translating Freud: Some Fundamental Questions

Lois Oppenheim

Lois Oppenheim

oppenheiml@montclair.edu

Lois Oppenheim  

Revision, interpretation, science

How does Mark Solms's Revised Standard Edition differ from James Strachey's Standard Edition and how is it similar? The objective of this paper is to explore not only the answers to these questions, but why they are important. Going beyond such questions in an effort to uncover the primary ethical, cultural, and methodological considerations pertaining to the Revised Standard Edition as well as their interconnection, this paper delves into the very meaning of translation and how it relates to psychoanalysis itself. Notions of the ‘untranslatable' and the ‘unknowable’ are discussed as well along with interdisciplinary implications stemming from philosophy, semiotics, and more.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytic Diaries of the COVID-19 Pandemic English Translation, by Pietro Roberto Goisis and Angelo Antonio Moroni, Abingdon, Oxfordshire, Routledge, 2022, 107pp., £21.99, ISBN: 978-1-032-05691-3

Jayne Hankinson

Jayne Hankinson

j.hankinson1@btinternet.com

Jayne Hankinson  

This evocative book outlines the thoughts and feelings, in diary form, of two Italian psychoanalysts during the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic: Angelo Antonio Moroni from the perspective of having to incorporate lockdown and working online into his private practice, alongside the impact of the pandemic on himself and his family; and Pietro Roberto Goisis from the perspective of being hospitalized with COVID-19 at the beginning of March 2020. The two parts are written very differently and the jointly authored introduction emphasizes these differences:

…the two diaries share a … graphic account of how …the epidemic forced Italian psychanalysts to reconsider … analytical “asymmetry” and “neutrality” - two different, but complementary points of view. (2)

The introduction also details a brief trajectory of the COVID-19 virus in Italy. I found myself taking note of the number of references to “first” - the first cases, the first confirmed death, the first “patient zero”, the first European country to be impacted - and wondered about reading something composed “in the moment” during March to July 2020 from the vantage point of 2023, having now a multitude of thoughts and feelings from my own experiences of working as an analyst during lockdown in a different country.

Moroni’s account spans ninety-one days, comprising free association to the events of his day as well as to the wider world. The shock of the unfolding situation, both personally and professionally, comes through in his entries for the first fifteen days or so. His language attests to this: the “battleground”, “I feel traumatised”, “war memories”, “medicine can do nothing” all feature on Day One (5).

I was struck by Moroni’s (perhaps) unconscious gender descriptions - the “hysterical nurses”, “hysterical mothers with children at home” (9), a doctor “who tells me that her mind is ‘too full’” (6, emphasis added), a male doctor patient of Moroni’s tells him that “a nurse doesn’t want … to work as she is tired of seeing dead people … ”. responseSet.title: The Older Analyst at Work: The Old Man and the Sea?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Older Analyst at Work: The Old Man and the Sea?

Eike Hinze

Eike Hinze

e.f.hinze@t-online.de

Eike Hinze  

Introduction

Thinking about my case history I remembered the famous story by Ernest Hemingway: the struggle of an old experienced fisherman for his captured marlin, which finally ends with sharks destroying his precious prey. Hemingway’s story does not serve as an allegory but rather symbolically expresses a characteristic of the analysis which I will report on below. In the following encounter with a patient an old analyst exposes himself to the depths of impending hopelessness and wrestles with the question of whether destructive dynamics will ultimately gain the upper hand.

I first saw the then thirty-eight-year-old patient, Mr A, in 2015, when I was seventy-five years old. Mr A came to the outpatient clinic of our psychoanalytic institute. A training candidate conducted the initial interviews. Due to legal regulations, I had to see the patient once. I then also took over the supervision of this initial interview process. Mr A had just completed a lengthy analysis and was suffering from a worsening sleep disorder. Clinical treatment had been unsuccessful. Three years later, Mr A contacted me again, having not found an analyst among the members of the Institute who was willing to take him into treatment.

This raises the question of whether I would have accepted him as a patient had I been younger. It was clear to me that it would be a long analysis which would go beyond the scope of the public health insurance. I would then only be able to continue the analysis at a significantly reduced fee. Would I have done the same as a young analyst who had to support a family and finance a house purchase? As an old, well-off and experienced analyst I felt more secure and could also fall back on something that I consider to be important. I was tempted to take on the challenge of what was probably a very difficult analysis with an uncertain prognosis and probably problematic countertransference entanglements.

Biographical Background

The patient was born as the only child of parents who both worked in the educational field. While the father is a conscientious, reliable man of obsessive-compulsive character, the mother seems to have been severely disturbed in her early development and object relations. She lost her own mother as a child, and was prone to paranoid reactions and depressive states. She herself told her son later on, that once when he was two years old, she had shied away from taking him in her arms, with the frightened exclamation: “Take him away. I cannot bear him.” She explained it as an effect of a depression. He

responseSet.title: Some Remarks on Freud as “Translator” and Translating Freud
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Remarks on Freud as “Translator” and Translating Freud

Heinz Weiss

Heinz Weiss

Frankfurt, Germany
weiss@sigmund-freud-institut.de

Heinz Weiss  

RSE, translation, transference, Nachträglichkeit, Zweizeitigkeit, epistemological conflict

The paper comments on Mark Solms’ Revised Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud and the challenges to grasp the subtleties and ambiguity of Freud’s language. It argues that “translation” is not something that just “occurs”’ to a text which is already completed, but an ongoing process that carries forward and explores different layers of meaning. The author tries to show that Freud saw himself as a “translator” and that “translation” reaches at the very core of the psychoanalytical endeavour. The multiple meanings of “transference”, and the translation of terms like “Nachträglichkeit” and “Zweizeitigkeit” (bi-temporality), are presented as examples of this. To conclude, Freud’s dwelling in two different epistemological models, the language of neurophysiology and the language of meaning, as well as the limits of translation are discussed.

responseSet.title: The Signification of the Principle of Constancy in Freud’s Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Signification of the Principle of Constancy in Freud’s Psychoanalysis

Pablo Lerner

Pablo Lerner

Götgatan 15, 116 46 Stockholm, Sweden
pablo.s.lerner@gmail.com

Pablo Lerner  

Freudian theory, epistemology, principle of constancy, “Project for a Scientific Psychology”, “Beyond the Pleasure Principle”

This article examines the signification of the principle of constancy in Freud’s pre-psychoanalytic drafts and papers and in Entwurf. It is argued that Freud’s principle differs from seemingly similar principles proposed by Breuer and Fechner, and that it constitutes an assumption about the maintaining of a constant amount of mobile biophysical energy whose purpose is not to return to equilibrium, but, proceeding from the primary functions of discharge (principle of inertia) and accumulation (exigencies of life), to consolidate an asymmetry within the nervous system. This gives rise to a set of quasi-psychological dualisms: an energetic dualism between kinetic and tonic energy; a systemic dualism between impermeable and permeable neurons; and a processual dualism between courses of the excitation with and without the inhibiting influence of the ego. Further, it is argued that the principle of constancy makes itself redundant, to the extent that it opens up a psychological domain operating relatively independent of its biophysical basis, thus enabling its “auto-subtraction” from the psychological theory. Lastly, it is argued that the constitution of psychoanalysis is dependent on an “epistemological repression” of this principle, and that “Beyond the Pleasure Principle” may be understood as the return of the repressed principle of constancy.

responseSet.title: Guest Editor's Introduction to the IJP Special Issue on Freud's Revised Standard Edition
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Guest Editor's Introduction to the IJP Special Issue on Freud's Revised Standard Edition

Mark Solms

Mark Solms

Cape Town, South Africa
Mark.Solms@uct.ac.za

Mark Solms  

As I explain in my Preface to the Revised Standard Edition: when the Institute of Psychoanalysis in London (the custodians of the Standard Edition) decided in 1989 to produce a revised version of it – in the wake of an international conference that they held on the topic – criticism of Strachey’s translation had reached fever pitch.

The Institute appointed a committee to oversee the project, under the chairmanship of Clifford Yorke, who was Anna Freud’s chosen successor when she retired from the Hampstead Clinic and who was the last editor of the International Psychoanalytical Library – which had been established in the 1920s by Ernest Jones, with the express purpose of disseminating Freud’s work in English, following the collapse of German and Austro-Hungarian hegemony at the end of the First World War. Other prominent members of the committee were Joseph Sandler, Pearl King and Riccardo Steiner. The fact that this committee appointed Albert Dickson to be the editor of the RSE indicates how modest the revisions were that they envisaged initially. Although Dickson assisted Angela Richards when she edited volume 24 of the SE, following Strachey’s death, he could not be described as a Freud scholar or translator. He was a copy-editor, albeit a very good one, who was known to Sandler by dint of the fact that he compiled the annual indexes of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis when Sandler was its editor.

The role assigned to me initially was likewise limited: to translate a few of Freud’s papers that they wanted to add to the RSE, such as some early lectures by him on hysteria, obsessional neurosis and phobia, his case-report on “Nina R”, an account of his own typical dreams, and some letters from him to Einstein and others. Dickson was asked also to include my translations of a few insignificant psychological papers by Freud that I had come across during my efforts to prepare his Complete Neuroscientific Works for publication. It is noteworthy that the Editor’s Introduction to Freud’s 'Overview of the Transference Neuroses' – the only major work by Freud that the committee intended to add to the RSE – was entrusted by them not to Dickson but rather to Ilse Grubrich-Simitis. Beyond inserting these additional translations at the end of the relevant volumes (so as to preserve the pagination of the old SE), Dickson’s editorial role involved little more than suppressing the hyphens in “psycho-analysis” and “to-day” and to incorporating Strachey’s own list of addenda and corrigenda into the main body of the texts.

responseSet.title: Traumatic Experience and Loss: A Brief Therapy with a Traumatized Refugee Boy and His Parents in Exile
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Traumatic Experience and Loss: A Brief Therapy with a Traumatized Refugee Boy and His Parents in Exile

Gertraud Diem-Wille

Gertraud Diem-Wille

Wien, Austria
gertraud.diem-wille@aau.at

Gertraud Diem-Wille  

Trauma in children, child therapy, post-traumatic stress syndrome, resilience, accompanying parental work

In this paper, the author portrays the psychoanalytic therapy with a twelve-year-old refugee boy and his parents, prior to which the boy had been traumatised by the deaths of both his brothers in the civil war. In 2015 he had travelled with his father to Austria, where he was warmly received in a small community. The author examines how this child reacted to the traumatising experiences, as well as which resilience factors played a role in overcoming them. The psychoanalytic process is illuminated in a detailed analysis of the therapy sessions, which created a space for overcoming the helplessness, mourning the loss and furthering the integration process of the identity, disturbed after the traumatic experiences.

responseSet.title: Discussion of “The Older Analyst at Work: The Old Man and the Sea?”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of “The Older Analyst at Work: The Old Man and the Sea?”

Audrey Kavka

Audrey Kavka

324 Pacific Avenue, Piedmont, CA 94611, USA
akavkamd@pacbell.net

Audrey Kavka  

It has been a pleasure to spend time with Dr Hinze’s paper. There is a bounty of introductory and concluding information in addition to the three reported analytic sessions that I approach with two aims: to elucidate how I see Dr Hinze working and to weave in commentary and questions that specifically focus on the analyst as an older man.

Dr Hinze begins the paper with a personal element, an association. The association is to Ernest Hemingway’s classic novel The Old Man and the Sea, which Dr Hinze pointedly summarizes as “the struggle of an old, experienced fisherman for his captured Marlin, which finally ends with sharks destroying and devouring his precious prey”. The choice to begin with an association, a thought that arises from both conscious and unconscious contributions, strikes me as important. It resonates with what he will stress in the following pages about his clinical technique characterized by analyst focus on transference–countertransference enactments, reverie and unconscious-to-unconscious communication.

Dr Hinze explains the intended message of the association “an old analyst exposes himself to the depth of impending hopelessness and wrestles with the question of whether destructive dynamics will ultimately gain the upper hand”. Dr Hinze sees a challenge for the analyst to contend with unintegrated aspects of the patient: the patient seeking help and the patient who acts like a destructive shark threatening the work with the analyst. The careful wording “destructive dynamics” that might “ultimately gain the upper hand” leaves open the possibility of the analyst’s destructive pressures also entering the transference–countertransference.

In the second paragraph, Dr Hinze introduces Mr A. as an applicant to the Institute Clinic. Mr A. had just terminated a “lengthy” (10 years, we later learn) analysis but was suffering with a worsening (long-standing) sleep disorder. The patient was 38 years old. Dr Hinze was 75 years old. They met once as part of a legal and clinic protocol. Three years pass, and Dr Hinze is contacted again by Mr A who has not been able to find one analyst “among the members of the Institute who was willing to take him into treatment”.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Aesthetic and Affective Matrix of Pre-Reflective Sensemaking at the Origins of the Relationship between Subject and World: A Dialogue between Kant’s Third Critique and Psychoanalysis

Giulia Tossici and Raffaele De Luca Picione

Raffaele De Luca Picione

Italy
r.delucapicione@unifortunato.eu
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-5951-8336

Benevento, Italy

Raffaele De Luca Picione  

Affects, aesthetics, fictionality, intersubjectivity, pre-reflectivity, psychoanalytical transitionality

The authors discuss the relevance of aesthetic and affective experience at the heart of the human being’s capability to relate to the world and to found relations of sense. Faced with anguish that the world can be meaningless and with fear of uncertainty/chaos, trust and hope are needed for the world to be a hospitable place for existence. Such experience is aesthetic, sensitive and affective before being rational, reflective and deliberative. Through a dialogue between Kant, Winnicott and Bion, it is shown how foundation of trust is based on two essential aspects: (1) The illusion that reality was created to allow us to live in it (namely, the fictionality is a prerequisite for each possible development of psyche) and (2) this illusion is not generated by a solipsistic activity of the human mind; rather, it is made possible starting from the primordial relationship with the other, by containing anguish, nourishing trust and hope, and supporting psychic development and elaboration of progressive forms of symbolisation. The authors discuss how these points have a profound aesthetic implication through deepening the reflection on the ontogenetic development of the psyche, the complex intertwining between primary and secondary processes, and clinical implications.

responseSet.title: Embodied Intersubjectivity: Forms of Psyche-Soma Structuring in the Encounter between Self and Other-Than-Self
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Embodied Intersubjectivity: Forms of Psyche-Soma Structuring in the Encounter between Self and Other-Than-Self**

Anna Ferruta and Maurizio Stangalino

Maurizio Stangalino

viale Bianca Maria 5.20122 Milano, Italy
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-6036-0348

Novara, Italy

Maurizio Stangalino  

Psyche-soma, primary mother–child relationship, neuroscience, life instinct, death instinct, intersubjectivity

This paper explores the mechanisms that lead to a destructive tendency in the formation and functioning of the psychic apparatus, to the characteristic states of subjects who are drawn to non-life. The dynamics of the primary mother-child relationship involve a structural interaction between mind and body, subject and object. The dialectic between the life drive and the death drive is conceptualized as the structuring of homeostatic dynamic equilibria, in which both drives belong to the living, provided they are kept in a non-isolated system. This conception has analogies with other disciplines that have changed their paradigms, such as neurobiology, which, for living beings in open systems, hypothesises a continuous interconnected Becoming of undivided separation and of discontinuity. In unitary psyche-soma functioning, a dynamic homoeostatic balance marks the state of health of the relating subject; or if, instead, the system is isolated, a pathological dysregulation depending on the emotional-affective vicissitudes it undergoes. Two clinical cases illustrate these dynamics. For this tendency on the level of the somatopsychic unit, the name alloiosis has been put forward, in analogy with cellular apoptosis.

responseSet.title: Discussion of A Candidate at Work: The Case of Ms. N.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of A Candidate at Work: The Case of Ms. N.

Marie G. Rudden

Marie G. Rudden

mgrudden@gmail.com

Marie G. Rudden  

I congratulate this candidate-analyst for demonstrating his work so that we might learn from it. The ability to show one’s work and to ask for help, especially with difficult patients, is of primary importance in our work as analysts. In treating patients like Ms. N., who is, by history, multiply traumatized, and who has an intense erotic transference, it is particularly crucial to open one’s work to other analysts’ thoughts, experience, and associations.

I must acknowledge that it can be difficult to discern what informs an analyst’s work from a report of only four sessions, the last two following a two-year period of treatment and occurring shortly before termination. Nonetheless, I was struck in these sessions by this analyst’s removing himself from his patient, which he describes as coming from discomfort with her curiosity about his life and her “stalking” him. This, he writes, made him “feel that I had to adopt an attitude as protective shield and neutral as possible.” Indeed, the patient’s emotionality, her confusion between her “stories” and reality, her fragile object relations, and her determined erotic transference would make this a particularly difficult case for a candidate to analyze. From the sessions, it does seem that this candidate-analyst works from a determinedly neutral stance, within a technique of analyzing in the “here-and-now” by making frequent observations aimed primarily at the transference. Despite his focus on what is happening immediately within the session, however, this analyst’s attempts at neutrality make it seem that he comes to the treatment from a distance, rendering him sometimes out of touch with his analysand’s experience. For example, during Session One, which follows the patient’s having seen the analyst outside the office with a woman, she remains silent for five minutes, crying, and then describes getting into bed with her father during childhood, lying on her side with her chest against his back, wishing he would turn to hold her, take away (and perhaps recognize and protect her from) her pain. Her analyst responds that she is worried about the intensity of her feelings “here”, and she answers that she wants to be able to fall apart and to have him “take responsibility … take care of things.” Her analyst queries: “For me to be like a mom?

responseSet.title: Discussion of A Candidate at Work: The Case of Ms. N.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of A Candidate at Work: The Case of Ms. N.

Paola Mariotti

Paola Mariotti

paola.mariotti@gmail.com

Paola Mariotti  

Reflecting on this presentation two very different situations come to my mind. First I was reminded of Bion writing about a meeting between two people – one could surmise that the people are an analyst and a patient:

When two personalities meet, an emotional storm is created. If they make sufficient contact to be aware of each other, or even sufficient to be unaware of each other, an emotional state is produced by the conjunction of these two individuals, and the resulting disturbance is hardly likely to be regarded as an improvement on the state of affairs had they never met at all. But since they have met, and since this emotional storm has occurred, the two parties to this storm may decide to “make the best of a bad job”. (Bion 1979, 247)

It is worth noticing that, for Bion, this storm happens in all situations, whether the two “personalities” are aware of meeting the Other, are aware of the significance and impact of that meeting and of what it represents for them, or are not aware of that significance and impact.

The second memory is a very personal recollection. It is a dream I had the night before I saw my first analytic patient. In the dream I was going to see somebody who was going to give me some important information. I did not know their precise address but it was apparently in my native city. Then I realized that I had lost my way, and finally I was not sure who the person was that I was going to visit, nor did I know what they were going to tell me, and it seemed that after all I was in an unknown city. I was worried and confused, feeling that really I should have been better prepared for my visit. I reported the dream to my analyst, who commented that I was going to have my first analytic session with a patient, and I was not sure of where I was going, what I would find, and how to get there. I knew she was right, but I did not like knowing that I was so inevitably under-prepared.

The analytic encounter is a unique experience, whether the analyst is a candidate or has many years of experience. Experience helps the analyst to be prepared for what one can know and especially for what one cannot know in the initial phase of a treatment, and to be at peace with that.

responseSet.title: Discussion of A Candidate at Work: The Case of Ms. N.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of A Candidate at Work: The Case of Ms. N.**

Abbot Bronstein

Abbot Bronstein

abbot.bronstein@gmail.com

Abbot Bronstein  

Charles Baekeland’s case, during the time he was a candidate, helps elucidate the quandary surrounding how we learn and what we attempt to teach. The additional problem that Charles’ case presents us with is how we teach the specifics of analytic thinking and technique when the cases candidates have during their training are “difficult.” Then the pain of the patient and the terrors and anxieties they bring into the clinical setting become an additional conundrum for candidates, supervisors, and institutes.

In an attempt to focus on the analyst-candidate at work, the endeavor is more complicated than looking at how an analyst “works.” A candidate work is in hopefully an evolutionary process, developed and developing at the same time. Thus, trying to think about the analyst’s internal working model, one may find a less stable, more flexible, or more rigid way of approaching clinical material. The influences of some unknowns, such as the candidates’ analysis, particular supervisors, and seminars all make the task we have here more complex. Yet we start with the basic assumption that the analyst-candidate and patient are involved in an analysis and analytic endeavor.

Candidates’ control cases have the great benefit of having two thoughtful clinicians looking at material over a long period of time. There is the luxury of observing, learning, and changing while having an additional “mind” helping them. It can also be confusing, and disorienting for all three participants as well. This is in addition to the fourth person involved, the analyst, and the institution as well.

Different theories of the mind, different characters, all approaching the material but not all for the same purpose, come together. The patient needs and wishes to get better, be less frightened, despairing, terrified of anxieties. The supervisor wishes to help the candidate learn to be an analyst. The candidate desires and needs to learn to do analysis. Patients disrupt us, tax us, and unsettle us as we learn to do this work.

responseSet.title: Transforming Psychoanalysis for the Public
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transforming Psychoanalysis for the Public

Pratyusha Tummala-Narra

Pratyusha Tummala-Narra

Chestnut Hill, MA, USA
tummalan@bu.edu

Pratyusha Tummala-Narra  

Sociocultural, Polarization, Community Engagment

This essay explores the inextricable connection between the psyche and the social, and its relevance to contemporary global challenges, such as isolation and polarization. The author discusses the possibilities that psychoanalysis holds for the public good, underscoring the application of psychoanalytic knowledge to understanding the social world and creating greater access to psychoanalytic knowledge. Notably, transforming psychoanalysis to reflect multiple sociocultural subjectivities and addressing the polarization within psychoanalysis is critical for this endeavour.

responseSet.title: In a Time of Theoretical Expansion and Change, Don’t Throw Babies Out with the Bath Water
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

In a Time of Theoretical Expansion and Change, Don’t Throw Babies Out with the Bath Water

Sally Weintrobe

Sally Weintrobe

London, UK
sallyweintrobe@btinternet.com

Sally Weintrobe  

methodology, climate change, theoretical development

Various challenges faced by the psychoanalyst when moving from “on the couch” work to “off the couch” work are raised and discussed. It is argued that the biggest challenges concern methodology: what now constitutes the analytic setting and field, and what counts as analytic data? The author describes some of the methodological challenges she has faced so far in studying climate change denial at individual, group, cultural and political levels. She raises potential pitfalls with “off the couch” work, that include overgeneralisations and assuming one can directly apply insights gained “on the couch” to wider contexts. In conclusion, she reflects that her training and practice working with individuals on the couch has proved bedrock in working “off the couch”.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Examination of a Case of “Treatment Failure” in Long-Term Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy for Treatment-Resistant Depression

Jochem Willemsen , Felicitas Rost , Marie Hustinx , Peter Fonagy and David Taylor

David Taylor

Place Cardinal Mercier 10/L3.05.01, Louvain-la-Neuve 1348, Belgium. +32/10.47.43.80
Jochem.willemsen@uclouvain.be
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4843-5209

Milton Keynes, UK
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-7250-9434

Louvain-la-Neuve, Belgium
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4084-0509

London, UK
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-0229-0091

London, UK

David Taylor  

Long-term psychoanalytic psychotherapy, treatment-resistant depression, repetition compulsion, treatment failure, object relations theory

Randomized controlled trials have reported psychoanalytic psychotherapy to improve longer-term post-treatment outcomes in patients with treatment-resistant depression. In this case study, we examine the therapy process of a female trial participant diagnosed with treatment-resistant depression. Structured clinical assessments indicated that the patient's level of depression remained unchanged during and after treatment. Over the course of the therapy, she repeatedly broke away from important others and finally also from the therapy itself, which we linked to the impact of earlier experiences of abandonment on her internal world. In the discussion, we present a variety of reflections that were put forward by the authors during a series of case discussion meetings. Some of these reflections relate to how the inner world of this patient might have triggered a negative therapeutic reaction and a destructive pattern of repetition. The interpretative stance, in which the therapist interpreted this reaction as indicative of a psychic conflict and linked this conflict to the therapeutic relationship, seemed to be experienced by the patient as unhelpful and persecutory. Other elements that were brought up include basic distrust, lack of symbolization and trauma in the patient, as well as the constraints of the research context.

responseSet.authorMast: Francisco J. González and  Lee Slome
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Splits in the Fabric: A Community Psychoanalytic Project During COVID-19

Francisco J. González and Lee Slome

Lee Slome

582 Market Street, Suite 706, San Francisco, CA 94104, USA
f.j.g@comcast.net

San Francisco, CA, USA

Lee Slome  

COVID, anti-Asian racism, community psychoanalysis, social unconscious

Demonstrating how psychoanalysts can be useful in community settings outside the conventional consulting room, this paper describes consultation and group interventions conducted at a San Francisco mental health agency serving a largely Asian community. In the traumatic context of the COVID-19 pandemic, agency staff became fragmented, due to remote working conditions and differential work assignments, including mandated deployments to emergency sites. Two psychoanalysts worked with agency leadership to devise a weekly process group held by video conferencing over 6 months, in an attempt to heal resentments and splits in the fabric of the agency. Examples of the group process, interventions, and major themes that emerged are described, as well as recommendations made, including the formation of an ongoing clinical consultation group. The paper situates these interventions in the greater context of the pandemic which exposed not only a universal threat to life and health, but also structural vulnerabilities organized along lines of (racial) difference and inequity. The dynamics at the agency are thus described as rooted within greater nested histories: of the clinic, its leadership, and their relationship with a strained public health system, and more broadly, of the tangled intersection of these histories with anti-Asian racism. These are understood as manifestations of the Social Unconscious, and the intervention as an example of Community Psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Editorial: IJP Special Issue on Freud's Revised Standard Edition
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial: IJP Special Issue on Freud's Revised Standard Edition

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

fjrgrier@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-852X

Francis Grier  

On hearing about the imminent publication of the Freud Revised Standard Edition, I immediately thought that the International Journal of Pyschoanalysis should mark this event which is bound to have special significance for the entire worldwide psychoanalytic community. I am aware, often painfully, of psychoanalysis becoming diffracted into so many different languages and discourses that the benefits of plurality can sometimes seem to become undermined by too much diversity. Might there be a single, unifying factor which joins together all these very different psychoanalysts and their conflicting theories?

The basic answer to this dilemma is the work of Freud. That surely is one element shared in common by all psychoanalysts. Psychoanalysts interpret Freud in radically different ways, but the basic texts of Freud are shared among us all. And, as we well know, there are an extraordinarily large number of those basic texts, since our founder thought and wrote copiously for so many years and on so great a number of different topics. In fact, you could say that the problem of psychoanalytic diversity starts within the ideas of Freud himself, since he not only had so many ideas, but his ideas were always developing and changing.

The next problem, however, is that Freud wrote in German, a language most analysts do not speak or read. It is for this reason that the translation of Freud into the almost universal language of English is of such paramount importance. Already the principle of a basic, unifying text begins to become more diffuse precisely because the text which most analysts use as “basic” is not actually Freud’s own original German text, but a translation.

And it is not any translation, but “the” translation. At this point we turn to the historians, who tell the completely fascinating human story of Freud’s early translators into English – A.A. Brill, Joan Riviere, Ernest Jones, Alix and James Strachey – leading to the monumental achievement of the Standard Edition. As time has moved on, that translation itself has been critiqued and questioned. Its revision has been in motion for some years – from 1994 to 2024 – but now Mark Solms’s name is to be added to the list as the next significant translator and editor of Freud, as the official Revised Standard Edition (RSE) has recently been processed and launched.

Roger Kennedy  

George Makari, in his wide-ranging and very readable book, examines the nature of stranger anxiety and hatred not by focusing on the extensive history and literature of tolerance, but by a comprehensive and fascinating excavation into the less known and more recent history of the use of the word “xenophobia”; by so doing he adds to our understanding of intolerant states of mind and provides a novel framework for trying to combat attacks on otherness:

By recovering its rich past, encompassing far more than an animus against immigrants, and by examining the numerous examples of stranger hatred to which it is linked, we may repurpose the term so that it serves to organize and promote attempts at synthesis. (237)

In his Prologue, Makari sketches out the personal journey which led to the writing of the book. His Lebanese parents eventually settled in the USA in 1955, although never felt completely at home there, often feeling strangers in their New Jersey environment. responseSet.title: Concerning the Nature of Psychoanalysis: The Persistence of a Paradoxical Discourse, by Gregorio Kohon, London and New York, Routledge, 2019, 140 pp., £ 36.99, ISBN: 978-0-367-23651-9
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Concerning the Nature of Psychoanalysis: The Persistence of a Paradoxical Discourse, by Gregorio Kohon, London and New York, Routledge, 2019, 140 pp., £ 36.99, ISBN: 978-0-367-23651-9

Richard Zimmer

Richard Zimmer

Great Barrington, MA, USA
richardbzimmermd@gmail.com

Richard Zimmer  

What is the essence of psychoanalysis? Although most analysts have at least an intuitive sense of what characterizes clinical work as “psychoanalysis” or, at least, “psychoanalytic”, it is not often explicitly elaborated. Yet today, psychoanalysts work increasingly in a world of social, political and economic upheaval, and face growing challenges from outside our field as to the value and relevance of our work. At the same time, in the current post-pluralistic era within our field, there is a growing need for clarity on this question as we attempt both to delimit the boundaries of psychoanalysis and to integrate multiple ways of understanding and working clinically with the human mind. Gregorio Kohon’s book Concerning the Nature of Psychoanalysis: The Persistence of a Paradoxical Discourse gives serious consideration to this question. Despite its relative brevity (there are 118 pages of text, including a preface, but not including its extensive bibliography), and the clear and extremely readable quality of Kohon’s prose, this is a book that is both deeply thoughtful and thought-provoking.

Kohon frames his argument as a response to challenges to psychoanalysis, particularly from within the public health sector, which rest on the discourse of evidenced-based medicine, with its underlying assumption of the universal value of operationalized procedures and outcomes measurable by quantitative means. What Kohon argues is that psychoanalysis achieves its clinical results through the establishment of a very particular kind of discourse between patient and analyst; that the results arise out of the process of the discourse itself rather than “answers” that are obtained through the discourse. The discourse is fundamentally a creative one rather than a problem-solving one, highly individual to each patient–analyst pair, and thus does not lend itself to being operationalized.

The clinical results, although they can be life-changing, are in the realm of qualitative differences in the patient’s experience of themself and of their relation to the external world, so that reducing these results to their quantifiable correlatives would tend to both distort and trivialize their actual nature. The nature of this discourse, how it works to produce clinical results and the nature of the clinical results themselves can best be understood, says Kohon, through the detailed single-case report, and not through statistical analyses of the kind of data that can be obtained through empirical research, which is the “evidence” of evidence-based medicine.

Laura Colombi

Laura Colombi

laurach.colombi@gmail.com

Laura Colombi  

In their introduction to the book Prendersi in gioco, Stefania Nicasi and Alessia Fusilli De Camillis write,

Prendersi in gioco is a distillation of Marta Badoni’s experience as a child neuropsychiatrist and a senior member and training analyst in the Italian Psychoanalytic Society (SPI), of which she has been National Secretary and Vice-President. The psychoanalyst recounts her personal story in a close interweaving of biographical, clinical, and institutional narratives. (xi)

I would like to use this as the starting point of my review because, as well as being a concise and thorough introduction to the author, it notes how one of the book’s substantial characteristics is this highly stimulating “interweaving” of different interconnected and mutually sustaining spheres. Rather like a personal travel diary, it is capable of prompting the reader into an individual focus on clinical, theoretical, and technical panoramas and educational and institutional experience: a “psychoanalytic treatise in a condensed form, but one of great clarity and depth”, as Giuseppe Di Chiara1 has called it. It is a “distillation of experience”, a “legacy for those who have time and inclination to draw upon it” (x), as the author herself calls it, one that unfolds in an “open, dynamic, and problematising” form (xii) – a “distillation of experience” that also, in its freedom of thought and the right doses of lightness and depth, its “flashes of intuition and blazing summaries” (xx), recalls features of writings by Nina Coltart, a psychoanalyst able to fuse personal and professional experience into a form of thought where, as in the essence of Marta Badoni’s work, what stands out as central is respect for the freedom of the subject. A respect for the other’s freedom to be himself or herself as parent, child, adult, pupil is the guiding principle of Prendersi in gioco and is combined with

Review translated by Adam Elgar

responseSet.title: Controversies in the English Publication of Freud’s The Interpretation of Dreams, 1913–1933
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Controversies in the English Publication of Freud’s The Interpretation of Dreams, 1913–1933

Roger Willoughby

Roger Willoughby

Birmingham
1 Manor Cottages, Valley Road, Barham, Canterbury, CT4 6PN, UK
rtwilloughby@gmail.com

Roger Willoughby  

The Interpretation of Dreams, censorship, translation, psychoanalytic politics

The transition of Freud’s Die Traumdeutung into its first English language incarnation as The Interpretation of Dreams, translated by A.A. Brill, took several years to effect, the process being beset with conceptual, practical, legal, and political obstacles. Anxieties over possible obscenity charges in Britain led to novel forms of censorship being applied to the first edition by its London publisher in 1913, while issues relating to the politics of translation and local British efforts to standardise psychoanalytic nomenclature would bedevil the text over its first two decades. In the context of the launch of the Revised Standard Edition (RSE), the present paper reveals these earlier issues for the first time. Having then considered the book’s reception in both Britain and the United States, its sales and varying editions, the paper concludes with a discussion of the covert and contentious reediting of the work’s third English edition, published in 1933. The shadow of this history inevitably falls on the RSE.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and its Discontents: A View from India

Sudhir Kakar

Sudhir Kakar

Sudhir Kakar  

Non-western civilisations, cultural imagination, bisexuality, separation

This paper discusses the ways in which psychoanalytic perspectives may have been limited by the Western cultural context in which they originated and explores the potential of the Indian cultural imagination to broaden psychoanalytic thinking about ego formation, the nature of Eros, bisexuality, and individuation. The case is made for the need to retain the diverse perspectives offered by the cultural imaginations of different civilisations despite the globalization of ideas.

responseSet.title: A Machine of the Same: Repetition in the Foundational Discourse of the Argentinean “being” (1976-1983)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Machine of the Same: Repetition in the Foundational Discourse of the Argentinean “being” (1976-1983)

Cecilia Taiana

Cecilia Taiana

Ottawa, Canada
Catherine.Humble@iopa.org.uk

Cecilia Taiana  

Structure of repetition, Mytho-Symbolic, anti-translation device

Using the example of the military regime in Argentina (1976-1983) and relevant archival materials, this article demonstrates the prerequisite of exalted language in constructing an enemy and how a discursive ‘machine of the same’ was put into operation. The author argues that what made this operation unique is its structure of repetition that stimulated “the tendency to merge” what is “foreigner-to-the-ego”, and the “enemy outside” into a single concept in the Argentinian national psyche.

As a theoretical lens, the author examines the military regime's language through Freud's understanding of groups and civilization and Laplanche's proposition that cultural narratives in the form of mytho-symbolic explanations help us translate the sexual drive and offer a “solution” to the helplessness of the infant-adult.

The author further claims that at other times a cultural narration functions as an anti-translation device when set against the emergence of a new net of significations. The nation's founding narrative of an Occidental-Spanish-Catholic “being” that first effaced its indigenous origins and then its Arabic and Jewish inheritance was brought back by the military regime as a mytho-symbolic narration that formed a shield against the repressed remnants of the enigmatic message pressing for a new translation.

responseSet.title: The Complexity of Psychoanalytic Language
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Complexity of Psychoanalytic Language

Gretchen Schmutz

Gretchen Schmutz

gschmutz@alumni.uchicago.edu

Gretchen Schmutz  

Dear Editor-in-Chief

The Letter to the Editor submitted by Tillotson, Winters and Harrang provides an opportunity for commentary about the complicated and confusing role of language in psychoanalysis. Their letter concerns a review by Michael Robbins of their book Body as Psychoanalytic Object: Clinical Applications from Winnicott to Bion and Beyond published in IJP, 2022, Volume 103, Issue 6.

Tillotson, Winters and Harrang outline numerous objections to Robbins’ review, but a main area of concern has to do with the diagnostic characterization and language used to describe the patients and interventions portrayed in the book’s clinical vignettes, which the authors feel mischaracterizes the focus of the book. These objections, along with a few other points, are used to argue that Robbins has a “broad misunderstanding of the focus of the book” (1) or alternatively, a “misreading” (p. 2).

A book review constitutes the thoughts and responses of a reviewer to a book and may be different from the wished-for response from the reader. Robbins offers both an appreciation of and a disagreement with this book. He writes, “The authors are articulate, poetic, and very knowledgeable on the aspects of object relations theory about which they write” (1133). While he sees things differently from some of the authors, he offers his own theoretical rationales for his disagreements.

The letter does, however, evoke two interesting questions: (1) how does the lack of uniformity of language, specifically definitions and terminology, affect the interpretation and understanding of ideas in psychoanalysis, and (2) are there situations where the conceptualization of a diagnostic genre of patients – characterizing patients along certain trajectories such as self–other differentiation, capacity for linguistic symbolization and other definitions alluding to the general level of psychic primitiveness of the patient – stands in the way of seeing a different psychoanalytic idea, as the letter purports. The issue of language becomes important here, in part, because Robbins’ review defines the meanings of his use of terms while the letter does not always do so.

Clinical Territories**

Magda Khouri

Magda Khouri

Rua da Consolação, 3741/22, 01416-001 São Paulo, SP, Brazil
magdakhouri@uol.com.br

Magda Khouri  

Extended clinic, community, institution, psychoanalytic listening, narrative

The author reflects on the extension of the psychoanalytic clinic, showing how ideas, studies and discussions built up over several years have led various Latin American groups to put their thinking into action, by allowing themselves to be penetrated by issues of the city and directly relate to diverse territories. These studies have shown how psychoanalytic actions can take place in various types of encounter between subjectivities, highlighting their challenges as well as their effectiveness and power.

responseSet.title: Historical-Critical Sigmund Freud Edition Meets Revised Standard Edition: Common Grounds, Differences and Interchange between Vienna and London
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Historical-Critical Sigmund Freud Edition Meets Revised Standard Edition: Common Grounds, Differences and Interchange between Vienna and London

Christine Diercks

Christine Diercks

christine.diercks@wpv.at

Christine Diercks  

Sigmund Freud, edition, philology

After 30 years of collective effort and intensive discussion, Mark Solms and the Institute of Psychoanalysis in London have finally succeeded in completing all 24 volumes of the Revised Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud (RSE). Within the framework of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society, we have been working for 12 years on the primarily digital historical-critical (historisch-kritische) Sigmund Freud Edition (FE). The FE strives for completeness. A look back in history also shows how closely the German and English editions are linked with each other and how deeply Freud himself was involved right up to the end of his life, and thus also authorised these editions. RSE and FE mark the current status of a development that is still ongoing. They have different approaches, tasks and foci; however, with regard to text-critical criteria, in many ways, they can support each other.

“My Father Says … My Father Says”: The Collaboration Between Sigmund and Anna Freud, James and Alix Strachey, when Translating the Case Histories

Riccardo Steiner

Riccardo Steiner

Retired Scholar, London, UK
ricst1945@aol.com

Riccardo Steiner  

History of psychoanalysis, translation, Sigmund Freud, Anna Freud

This paper tries to study the complexity of the personal and professional relationship between S. and A. Freud and A. and J. Strachey when they were translating Freud's Case Histories, starting with the years they spent in Vienna immediately after the end of the first world war being both in analysis with Freud and were checking the translations with him and his daughter. Those unique meetings according to me influenced even many aspects of Strachey SE later on and have still to be kept in mind even today as far as Mark Solms Revision of the SE is concerned. I also try to show the importance and the final control of the Strachey's translation exerted by E. Jones who had planned the Standard Edition of Freud' work already in 1920 and create a very rigid glossary in order to translate him which the Strachey's had to apply to their translations. I have used published and unpublished letters and notes on the translation to try to prove what I just stated.

responseSet.title: Loss and Memorialization
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Loss and Memorialization

Lucy LaFarge

Lucy LaFarge

41 West 96th St., New York, NY 10025, USA
lucyblafarge@gmail.com

Lucy LaFarge  

Loss, mourning, memorialization, termination

Drawing upon Dana Birksted-Breen's work on temporality, and the contrast which she draws between linear, developmental time, seen as a series of unchanging moments, and bidirectional, process time, which is retranscribed again and again (Birksted-Breen [2003] 2016. “Time and the Apres-Coup.” In The Work of Psychoanalysis, edited by D. Birksted-Breen, 139-157. London: Routledge.), the author considers the role of unchanging elements in mental life. She argues that these elements are a form of memorialization, which defends against loss. Incorporated throughout life, they have a core of concrete, sensory experience but evoke larger scenes that are not entirely sensory. The author explores the operation of two kinds of memorial tokens: screen memories, which remain inalterable, and recurrent dreams whose unchanging structure serves as a frame in which change can take place. In analysis, both of these preserve memories of the analytic process and re-evoke the presence of the analyst. Both are particularly prominent at termination, when they function both as markers of approaching termination and as defenses against the loss of the analyst. Two brief clinical examples from patients who are nearing termination Illustrate these points.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Hauntology of Everyday Life, by Sadeq Rahimi, 2021, Palgrave Macmillan, London, £54.99, ISBN: 978-3-030-78991-6, 106 pp.

Stephen Frosh

Stephen Frosh

London, UK
s.frosh@bbk.ac.uk

Stephen Frosh  

Psychoanalysis is a haunted discipline and a discipline of haunting. It is haunted by various elements of its own history – Freud’s biography, the Jewishness of most of its founders, its early involvement with the occult, the Nazi persecution of psychoanalysis, its dissensions and internecine antagonisms, and so on – some of which have been addressed, others of which keep returning in one way or another to trouble its present-day standing. More obviously, it concerns itself with a kind of restlessness in its adherents and in its clients – analysands – infected by pasts that will not leave them alone and that well up often unbidden, and sometimes outside of conscious awareness, in and outside the transference. For Freud, writing in the Little Hans case study, psychoanalysis was precisely a way of dealing with ghosts: “In an analysis … a thing which has not been understood inevitably reappears; like an unlaid ghost, it cannot rest until the mystery has been solved and the spell broken” (Freud 1909, 123). In this sense, psychoanalysis can be seen as what Sadeq Rahimi, in The Hauntology of Everyday Life, calls a procedure of “exorcism,” aiming to remove the ghosts, to let them stop their wanderings and infiltrations of the present, their possessions of the innocent, and lay them to rest. A ghost is the remains of something that is unsettled because a task has not been completed; specifically, as Avery Gordon (1997) notes in her classic text on the topic, an injustice has 1106

Analysis and Exile: Boyhood, Loss and the Lessons of Anna Freud, by Vivian Heller, 2022, Confer Books, London, €18.99, ISBN: 978-1-913494-36-0

Fred Busch

Fred Busch

Newton, MA, USA
drfredbusch@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-1457-3198

Fred Busch  

This book is the story of Peter Heller, a patient of Anna Freud’s, and an examination of Anna Freud’s experiment with applying psychoanalytic principles to the treatment and education of children in the late 1920s.1 It is a sprawling history of this time, alongside the focus on Peter Heller, and Anna Freud’s treatment of him.2 Peter’s analysis started when he was nine, and he also attended the Heizing school, founded by Anna Freud and Dorothy Burlingham. The children and teachers in this school played an important role in Peter’s development. In addition, in the background of Peter Heller’s adolescence, there was the rise of the Nazis in Germany, which eventually spread to Austria and affected everyone. The author beautifully captures this era.

Vivian Heller is in a special place to write about Peter Heller as she is his daughter, and an author of well-received books of history (Heller 1996, 2004). Further, Peter Heller was an 1All citations to page numbers without reference to the author’s name are to be understood as from the book under review.2As Midgley (2012) notes, the first phase of Peter’s analysis lasted 15 months, and ended in 1930. After 5 months Peter returned to treatment with Anna Freud when his mother had a mental breakdown.1109

responseSet.title: How can Psychoanalytic Interpretations of Political Situations have Effects as Actions?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How can Psychoanalytic Interpretations of Political Situations have Effects as Actions?

Michael Rustin

Michael Rustin

3A Exeter Road, London NW2 4SJ, UK
m.j.rustin@uel.ac.uk

Michael Rustin  

Interpreting political phenomena, John Maynard Keynes, The Inability to Mourn

This article examines the difficulties of making use of psychoanalytic insights to understand and influence political events. In clinical practice, it has often been possible to bring about understanding and change in patients, and in that context, immense developments in psychoanalytic theories and techniques have taken place. But there is no parallel tradition giving rise to the interpretation of unconscious political phenomena although there have been outstanding contributions of this kind by individuals, beginning with Freud's work on group psychology. There have been valuable psychoanalytic understandings of broad social changes, but effective interventions in “here and now” political situations have been few. Some examples of these include Keynes's understanding of the economic consequences of the peace of 1918 which were seen to be relevant mainly after the later peace of 1945 and Mitscherlichs' analysis in the1970s of the German people's “inability to mourn” the catastrophes of the Nazi period. The article concludes with reflections on the conditions which might facilitate effective interpretations of political situations by psychoanalysts today.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Omnipotent State of Mind: Psychoanalytic Perspectives edited by Jean Arundale, London and New York, Routledge, 2022, 239 pp., £ 31.99, ISBN: 978-1-032-02794-4

Ricardo Stramer

Ricardo Stramer

London, UK
ricardostramer12@gmail.com

Ricardo Stramer  

The book begins with the working hypothesis that omnipotence is a concept as well as a mechanism. Emphasizing that the topic remains undeveloped as a metapsychological subject, Jean Arundale proposes to study this through the clinical experiences of psychoanalysts of differing orientations. Defining omnipotence as a phantasy by the ego, she asks the following questions: Where does omnipotence originate, what purpose does it serve and why is it so difficult to relinquish? The book is divided into three sections: the first concentrates on the chronological appearance of omnipotence, the second deals with the difficulty in relinquishing omnipotence, and the final section examines the influence of omnipotent thought in the wider psycho-social sphere.

The first three chapters, under the section title “In the Beginning … ”, emphasize the point that omnipotence is a natural state. Joan Symington, in her paper entitled “The Survival Function of Primitive Omnipotence”, based on baby observations at the Tavistock Clinic, argues that it is necessary to differentiate the fear of physical disintegration from a more psychological sense of fragmentation and opens the debate of the frontier between the soma and the psyche as well as the dilemma of the origins of anxiety. Lesley Steyn, in “The Thumb-in-the-Mouth Phantasy and the Capacity to Love”, demonstrates how her patient needed to regress to a “garden of Eden” to hide from her shame of not being able to love. Steyn argues that omnipotence arises as a narcissistic regressive response to a lack of belief in one’s own life force that stems from a failure in the developing environment. Kathleen Kelley-Lainé, in “Peter Pan, the Omnipotent Child”, reinforces an idea similar to those of the two previous authors that omnipotence is a natural state of affairs for the human infant. Through a presentation of an adult patient who survived the holocaust as a child, she bases her hypothesis on the premise that there is such a thing as primary narcissism where the child feels it is the centre of the universe. However, some readers might wonder whether the wish to remain in a “Peter Panesque” type of world is based not just on a form of indulgence but frequently also on a fear of encountering the dangers that appear after Pandora’s box is opened. Contributors and Reviewers for 2023

The Editors, Francis Grier, Lesley Steyn, Bruce Reis, Bernard Reith, Beatriz de León de Bernardi, Georg Bruns, Sara Boffito, and Catherine Humble would like to express their gratitude to the team of reviewers throughout the world responsible for the successful implementation of the Journal’s peer-review process. These colleagues devote a great deal of time and effort in the service of providing detailed and thoughtful evaluations of manuscripts, film essays and book reviews which will ultimately help the authors strengthen their contributions. We would also like to thank the Section Editors for all their hard work: Heinz Weiss (Education Section), Elizabeth Allison (Psychoanalytic Controversies), Abbot Bronstein (Analyst at Work), Georg Bruns (Psychoanalysis in the Community), Bruce Eadie (Film Essays), Sara Collins (Book Reviews), Gretchen Schmutz (North American Book Reviews), and all our Book Review Associates. We would also like to thank their Editorial teams. Without the dedication of the Section Editors and their teams, along with the reviewers, the IJP would not have achieved the scholarly status it enjoys, and we would like to thank them for everything they have done to assist us during the past 12 months. In addition, we would like to thank the Editors and Board Members of the IJP Annuals who produce collections of IJP papers in various languages. We wish to express our appreciation to our translators who carry out essential work for the Journal’s aims. Last but not least, we would like to thank Chris Lawrence our Journal Assistant for his hard work behind the scenes. We thank the following reviewers:

responseSet.title: Sudhir Kakar (1938–2024): Dancing to the Rhythms of Empathy and Imagination
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sudhir Kakar (1938–2024): Dancing to the Rhythms of Empathy and Imagination

Honey Oberoi Vahali

Honey Oberoi Vahali

honey@aud.ac.in

Honey Oberoi Vahali  

Dr Sudhir Kakar was India’s most celebrated contemporary psychoanalyst. He was also an acclaimed cultural and post-colonial thinker. Dr Kakar has impacted the global understanding of unconscious processes in individual and collective life. His influence in psychoanalysis, literature and religious and spiritual studies, and his analytical method of studying cultures and civilisations, is worth engaging with. In his later years he moved beyond his life-long preoccupation of studying the culture-and-psyche relationship to that of the psyche-within-civilisational-flows. As a psychoanalytical practitioner his insights were contextually grounded, compassionate and astute. It was the relational aspect of psychoanalysis that involved him the most. This helped him identify with both the position of the analyst and the patient, the victim as well as the victimiser. He appealed for sensitivity in understanding complex actions and situations by retaining a concern for the human condition of perpetrators too.

As a psychoanalytical researcher, he preferred the longitudinal span offered by the life historical and psychobiographical method. Inspired by Erik Erikson, in his prolonged search for an androgynous and non-violent human aesthetic he involved himself in the biographical analysis of major historical figures from India such as M. K. Gandhi, Rabindranath Tagore and Ramakrishna Paramahamsa among others. These immersive sojourns into life histories enabled him to think of peace and love without projecting and splitting off currents of aggression and violence in self and others.

Of central interest to him was the promise of enlivenment that Eros and sexual desire offered on the one hand, and on the other hand that of inner stillness and quietude which a spiritual inclination could potentially awaken. I have often considered him as a thinker of the “And” rather than of the “Or” as throughout his life Sudhir Kakar strived to bridge differences and open dialogical spaces between seemingly incompatible social binaries. As an illustration, he considered that sexuality, desire, love, madness and spirituality all lay on a continuum and were expressed in our intimate attempts of relatedness with self and others. Similarly, an exploration of the possible interconnectedness, without *Honey Oberoi Vahali is a Psychoanalyst, Clinical Psychologist and Professor of Psychology at the School of Human Studies, Dr B. R. Ambedkar University Delhi (AUD), India. She is the founding Director of the Centre of Psychotherapy and Clinical Research, AUD. She has actively contributed in bringing socially sensitive psychoanalytic knowledge and practice in the context of Higher Education in India.

[[RunningHead]]

Marie Bonaparte, Sigmund Freud Complete Correspondence (1925–1939) edited by Rémy Amouroux, Translation (German): Olivier Mannoni, Paris, Flammarion, 2022, 1071 pp., €42, ISBN: 9782080264572

Emmanuelle Chervet

Emmanuelle Chervet

emmanuelle@chervet.fr

Emmanuelle Chervet  

The Complete Correspondence between Sigmund Freud and Marie Bonaparte was published in French in October 2022. The historian Rémy Amouroux had written his thesis in 2012, using the publicly accessible archives of Marie Bonaparte, which were deposited at the Bibliothèque nationale de France (Amouroux 2012). He then consulted the Marie Bonaparte Collection of the Sigmund Freud Library of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society (PPS). While working with Cécile Marcoux, director of the Sigmund Freud Library and archivist of the Marie Bonaparte Collection at the PPS, he anticipated the opening to the public of the Marie Bonaparte Collection at the Library of Congress, scheduled for 2020, when he immediately went to consult it, and discovered the complete correspondence of more than 900 letters, including those of Freud mainly in German, as well as Marie Bonaparte’s diary. Cécile Marcoux, who had a working relationship with HRH Tatiana Radziwill, the granddaughter of Marie Bonaparte, was able to obtain the rights for an academic monograph to be published by Flammarion. An expert professional translation was provided by Olivier Mannoni,1 and an in-depth critical apparatus to contextualize the letters was developed by Rémy Amouroux.

1Olivier Mannoni is the translator of numerous writings and correspondence of Freud, founder of the École de Traduction Littéraire and winner of the 2018 Eugen-Helmé French–German Translation Prize.624
