responseSet.title: Letter from Florence: European Psychoanalytic Federation – 37th Conference – Florence, Italy, 22–24 March 2023
responseSet.authorMast: Benedetta Guerrini Degl’Innocenti and  Chiara Matteini
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter from Florence**

European Psychoanalytic Federation – 37th Conference – Florence, Italy, 22–24 March 2023

Benedetta Degl’Innocenti and Chiara Matteini

Chiara Matteini

Florence, 50137, Italy
guerrinib@gmail.com

Florence, Italy

Chiara Matteini  

Italian psychoanalysis, Italian psychoanalytic institutions, History of Italian psychoanalysis

It is a fascinating decision to hold a conference on identifications in Florence. Now identified for more than five centuries with a brief portion of its millennial story, it sometimes seems imprisoned in its apparently timeless beauty, which is in fact extremely fragile and precarious. In this alternation of isolation and openness Florence is undoubtedly an ideal representation of Italian history. In the end, the characteristics of the development of psychoanalysis in Italy could also be summed up in the oxymoronic pairing of Isolation and Openness. It is thanks to this evolutionary niche that linguistic isolation has been transformed from a difficulty into an advantage. Italian psychoanalysis is a blend of different psychoanalytic traditions interpreted and retranslated into an autonomous psychoanalytic language. If we wish to trace a common feature in Italian psychoanalysis we could point to the concept of relationship as one of its most important transformations, with a shift of focus onto the work of the analytic couple. “Psyche is extended, knows nothing about it”, the later Freud's most enigmatic legacy, seems to be one of the compasses for contemporary psychoanalytic research in Italy, one that spins around extensions of practice and the widening of technique.

This book presents the continuing work by the IPA Committee on Clinical Observation (Altmann de Litvan 2014) in developing new methods for studying the psychoanalytic process. The observational research uses a specific methodology, the “Three-Level Model” (3-LM), based on psychoanalytic cases presented to small groups in which over 1000 analysts from around the world have participated. [[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis at the crossroads: An international perspective, edited by Fred Busch, Oxon and New York, Routledge, 2023, pp. 326, £23.99 (paperback), ISBN: 978-1-032-37550-2

Kristin White

Kristin White

Kristin.White@t-online.de

Kristin White  

Fred Busch has collected papers from well-known authors from North and South America, Europe and Israel with very different ideas concerning the crossing of paths in psychoanalysis in the present and looking towards the future.

Busch describes, in his short introduction, the process of the book's development, in which he asked well-known psychoanalytic authors to write on the topic of psychoanalytic crossroads. Busch declares “I’m someone who prefers we don’t drift into going one way or the other when we reach the crossroads. Lively debate of these issues, I believe, is our main task” (3). Busch, it seems, would like us to stand at the crossroads and listen to the varying points of view presented in the chapters of this book and to discuss these views, with the hope of finding some common ground. Busch also expresses a concern that he has observed an increasing tendency of analysts to retreat into their chosen group or school of psychoanalysis, “surround[ing] ourselves with people who agree with our conclusions, when we should be gravitating towards those who challenge our thought process” (1).

The task for this book and for the future of psychoanalysis, is thus, according to Busch, a “lively debate” of controversial issues. However, this is a book with such a wealth of discussion material from so many different and mostly opposing corners of the psychoanalytic arena that it seems to be more of a spaghetti junction than a crossroads. Because each chapter stands alone, presenting specific ideas that are often clothed in the language of a specific school of psychoanalysis, the “lively debate” is often indiscernible. Nonetheless, I agree with Busch that it is the “lively debates” and the controversial discussions that make up the “treasures” of psychoanalysis, in the past and to this day.

The first section of the book, “What is Basic About Psychoanalytic Technique and Theory”, concentrates mostly, but not exclusively, on controversies stemming from North America: the relational, interpersonal and intersubjective branches of psychoanalysis that have developed 442

responseSet.title: Response to the Editors of Body as Psychoanalytic Object
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to the Editors of Body as Psychoanalytic Object

Michael Robbins

Michael Robbins

mdrobbinsmd@comcast.net

Michael Robbins  

The editors of Body as Psychoanalytic Object express their displeasure with my review of their book using the language of labelling and disqualification rather than that of respectful disagreement. They assert that I have not written what they characterize as a “legitimate” review, that the review is “odd,” that it is a “misreading,” and that I have made errors. The review was published a year ago, so I hope the interested reader will read it before forming an opinion about the criticism as what follows only addresses particular assertions in the letter, not the sweeping generalizations that characterize much of it.

The substance of the objections is not clear. The editors believe that I do not understand what the body as psychoanalytic object means, and that I do not understand the kind of body-to-body communication they describe. They write: “each vignette presents the body as a unique type of psychoanalytic object for our analytic attention.” They assert the presence of what they call “body-to-body communication in the analytic couple.” And they write about “the body’s capacity to communicate its state through dreaming,” suggesting that they view “the body” as an entity separate from the thoughtful self.

When they write “Robbins overlooks Eckhoff’s thesis that dreaming starts in the body,” they do not realize that I wrote about dreaming in 2015, elaborating and revising Freud’s model based on the primary process mind which is basically somatic and enactive.

In support of the idea that the body is an autonomous communicating entity, they cite this passage from Bion’s Learning from Experience: “it is with the detection and observation of these [psychoanalytic] objects that the psychoanalyst must concern himself in the conduct of an analysis (1962, p. 68).” This conveys the false impression that Bion’s reference to a psychoanalytic object refers to the body. In fact, Bion’s book – especially this section – consists of his mathematical-logical schema to account for how thought develops from conversion of beta (sensory-perceptual-somatic) elements to alpha elements. In it the term “concept” might be substituted for “object.” In fact, the word “body” is found only three times in the entire book, none of these in reference to the human body.

Steven Groarke

sgroarke2@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-3203-3494

Steven Groarke  

How can the dying forgive the living their happiness? This is one of the many intriguing and difficult questions raised in Psychoanalytic Approaches to Forgiveness and Mental Health (2024), an outstanding collection of twelve essays edited by Ronald Britton and Aleksandra Novakovic. The contributors comprise a distinguished group of analysts, including major representatives of the Kleinian tradition. It is not possible here to do justice to the entire collection; moreover, among the most innovative, if not idiomatic voices in the volume, the chapters by Karl Figlio, Francis Grier and Ignês Sodré do not invite paraphrase. Despite these provisos, I believe the quality and diversity of the collection will be self-evident in my comments.

In the opening chapter, Novakovic has interesting things to say about early infantile life, Oedipal conflict, and loss and death as everyday domains of forgiveness. She introduces several stimulating lines of inquiry, including: the act of forgiveness and the survival of the object at the beginning of life; the integration of different parts of the self (injured and injuring; offended and offender; repentant and impenitent); and the idea of forgiveness as a way of showing mercy. Novakovic gives us a lot to think about. Together with the idea that compassion and clemency embody a forgiving disposition, her emphasis in the opening chapter 446

The Claustro-Agoraphobic-Dilemma in Psychoanalysis. Fear of Madness, 1st Edition, edited by S. Finkelstein and H. Weiss, 2022, Routledge, Oxon and New York, £39.95, ISBN: 978-1-032 06013-2 (hbk), ISBN: 978-1-032 06014-9 (pbk), 978-1-032 06018-4 (ebk)

Judy Eekhoff

Judy Eekhoff

jkeekhoff@comcast.net

Judy Eekhoff  

The Claustro-Agoraphobic Dilemma in Psychoanalysis: Fear of Madness, edited by Susan Finkelstein and Heinz Weiss, is a uniquely organized exploration into the history and development of the treatment of patients whose internal object relations are confused and confusing. These analysands seek relationship even as their defences against emotional intimacy war against obtaining it. The fear of being forever stuck inside the object wars with the fear of being forever alone. The war itself is demonstrated via a repetitive phenomenon of broken relationships and disappointments, in part due to an inability of the patient to discern what is real. In addition, the war evokes set narratives that in and of themselves maintain the dilemma. Of course, these are replayed within the analytic dyad. Their dilemma leaves them in constant psychic pain. It often also leaves their analysts baffled.

The editors have taken a distinctive stance in their editing by providing the reader with a theoretical and meta-psychological context for the development of Henri Rey’s 1979 coining of the term “claustro-agoraphobic dilemma”. This term names and marks a stuck place within the analysand that impedes development. Often, this clinical dilemma reflects an impasse that leaves both the analyst and the analysand confused, frustrated and fearing madness. The placement of Rey’s paper in the centre of the book enables the reader to approach his ideas about conflictual intimacy in a spatial manner, much as Rey himself approached object relations – as a function of the relation to and movement within the psychic space of self and object. The concept of psychic dimensionality is also investigated by the editors’ choice of papers and their introductions to them. This editorial stance parallels the position analysts often take when attempting to discover what is happening in the analytic relationship. We are both participating and observing as we contextualize the emotional experience of the hour. In doing so, we are inside the process even as we are coming to recognize and understand it. In addition, Finkelstein and Weiss order their choices so as to contextualize these relational dilemmas within a fear of madness. They also provide papers from current analysts that demonstrate how these insights are used with today’s analysands.

responseSet.title: Editorial: Remembering Dana Birksted-Breen
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial: Remembering Dana Birksted-Breen

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

fjrgrier@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-852X

Francis Grier  

It is with the greatest sadness that I write this editorial following the death of Dana Birksted-Breen, Editor-in-Chief of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis (2007–2022), on 1 June 2024.

Through her 15 years of editing the International Journal as well as the New Library of Psychoanalysis (2000–2010), Dana Birksted-Breen became arguably the most experienced and influential psychoanalytic editor in the world. Self-effacing almost to a fault, she was particularly generous in promoting others, especially in helping authors to improve their submissions to a standard of which they hadn’t realised they were capable. Her own papers were marked by a special blend of creative imagination and sensitivity allied with an encyclopaedic grasp of the theories of classical and contemporary psychoanalysis. This is a sad day for the Journal and her colleagues, past and present, to whom she dedicated so much attention and affection. Writing personally, I can say without any doubt that I owe my own editorial development in very large part to Dana. My experience will be shared by many.

I wish to pay tribute to the sheer quality and quantity of Dana’s contribution to the International Journal. As a board member, she used to be the Book Review Editor, then she become the joint Editor-in-Chief with Robert Michels for five years, before she went on to become the sole Editor-in-Chief, a role which she held for 10 years. So all in all, the journal benefited from Dana as Editor for 15 years. This is quite an amazing achievement. Under her stewardship, the Journal continued to set the highest standards, but also inquired into all sorts of new trends within psychoanalysis, and particularly lived up to its name of being international. Dana was always keen to explore and encourage writing from many different areas of the world in all sorts of different psychoanalytic dialects. Indeed, translation was at the core of her interests, the subject of translation, in its very many ramifications and broadest aspects, perhaps at its most elemental the translation of the unconscious into consciousness. The book she edited and inspired about the centenary of the International Journal was entitled Translation/Transformation, opening with a wonderful essay she contributed on that theme, and how it has rolled out in the history of the IJP. Since retiring as Editor, she also worked some of the time translating French papers for the Journal, together with her husband Ian Birksted, who supported her throughout her tenure as Editor, as well as through her final illness.

responseSet.title: Rethinking the Concepts of the Unconscious and Analytic Time
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Rethinking the Concepts of the Unconscious and Analytic Time

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

306 Laurel Street, San Francisco, CA 94118
thomas.ogdenmd@gmail.com

Thomas Ogden  

Conscious, unconscious, consciousness, diachronic time, synchronic time, childhood trauma

The author proposes ways of rethinking the concepts of the unconscious and time in the analytic setting, including the very existence of the unconscious. Freud (1915) stated that the success psychoanalytic thinking has in making inferences about the patient’s unconscious makes the existence of the unconscious “incontrovertible.” The author submits that this success does not establish the existence of the unconscious; rather, the inferences we think we make about the unconscious are inferences about consciousness itself - the totality of our experiences of thinking, feeling, sensing, observing, and communicating with ourselves. The author then offers thoughts about a second analytic concept, the experience of time in the analytic setting. He conceives of there being two inseparable sorts of experiences of analytic time that stand in a dynamic relationship with one another: diachronic time (clock time) and synchronic time (dream time). In diachronic time, time is sequential; one thing leads to another. In synchronic time, all time is contained in the present. In analysis, childhood trauma is experienced for the first time (in synchronic time) in the co-created subjectivity of patient and analyst.

responseSet.title: The Inability to Mourn: Past and Current Challenges for Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Inability to Mourn: Past and Current Challenges for Psychoanalysis

Anna Christopoulos

Anna Christopoulos

Athens, Greece
annachr@otenet.gr

Anna Christopoulos  

Global mourning difficultites, social antecedents, social consequences

Although the capacity to mourn is ubiquitously acknowledged as critical for individual psychic functioning, the impact of this capacity on a collective social level has been examined to a very limited extent in the psychoanalytic literature to date. The two papers that take up this this topic thus bring various critical and complex issues to our attention. After reviewing and commenting on these papers, I discuss how these issues are particularly relevant today to society in general and psychoanalysis in particular. I believe that the ability to mourn is under siege in the Western world at present, with respect to both “macro” mourning that is, mourning for significant losses such as a beloved person, ideal, or country, and “micro” mourning or mourning for losses inherently and unavoidably implicated in choices we make in everyday life. These mourning processes are undermined by the impact of complex socioeconomic parameters on psychic functioning, as evidenced by various internal problems and symptomatology characteristic of our times. In turn, difficulties in mourning contribute to social problems including social injustice, wars and the climate crisis. As psychoanalysts we are called upon to address these issues in our clinical work as well as in our global community.

[[RunningHead]]

National Myths and the Inabilities of Nations to Mourn and to Atone: An Essay Prompted by “The Inability to Mourn and Nationalism in Japan after 1945”, and “A Machine of the Same: Repetition in the Foundational Discourse of the Argentinian ‘being’ (1976-1983)”

Marie Rudden

Marie Rudden

West Stockbridge, MA, USA
mgrudden@gmail.com

Marie Rudden  

National myths, mourning in large groups, intergenerational trauma

Introduction

The two papers that follow this essay use different modes of psychoanalytic investigation to consider the impact of significant national traumas on their countries’ citizenry. I use these papers as an invitation to comment further on the different kinds of silence wrought by the terror described in these papers, on the difficulties inherent in mourning traumatic events, and on the ways in which national myths can defend against the pain of traumatic loss (loss of pride and of identity) and against guilt and shame about the perpetration of trauma. I examine, as does Vamik Volkan (1997), how such myths, especially about national or regional identity, may perpetuate violence. I offer examples too of the discourse derived from myths that deny loss or guilt, and discuss the the intergenerational impact of the denying trauma and perpetration. Finally, I consider these themes in an example from recent United States history.

Silence during and After Traumatic Events

“The inability to mourn and nationalism in Japan after 1945” traces the history of dual traumas: first, the perpetration of well-known atrocities by the Japanese state during the Second World War; and, second, the mass victimization of the Japanese people through nuclear assaults on Hiroshima and Nagasaki. The authors describe an eerie cultural silence extant in the nation since 1945 on matters of socio-political and historical significance, and its replacement by a modern Japanese identity rooted largely in its economic system. They contend that this vast silence, along with the displacement of the national identity towards a manic striving for economic success, was caused by three factors: a cultural inability to mourn Japan’s defeat in that war, the devastation of nuclear assault on two of its major cities, and the failure to confront and mourn the atrocities its state committed during that time. They also attribute it to a void created within Japanese society after the post-war occupying Allied Forces insisted the emperor issue a “Humanity Declaration”, declaring to his subjects that he was not a god, as the State

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Setting: José Bleger’s Encuadre
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Setting: José Bleger’s Encuadre

John Churcher

John Churcher

churcher@aulos.co.uk

John Churcher  

Bleger, setting, body

José Bleger’s paper on the setting (encuadre) is integral to his 1967 book Symbiosis and Ambiguity. Relevant concepts from the book are summarised before examining his view of the setting as a “non-process” consisting of “constants”, complementing the “variables” of the analytic process. Process and setting are related as figure and ground in Gestalt psychology. The ideally maintained setting is studied as a thought experiment, uniting the categories of institution, personality, body schema, and body. Deposited in the setting, the psychotic part of the personality, or “agglutinated nucleus”, is a remnant of early symbiosis with the mother. Bleger distinguishes two settings: the analyst’s and the patient’s. The latter can only be analysed by strictly maintaining the former. Ritualisation of the setting denies temporal reality. De-symbiotisation is not always possible. A concept of “internal” setting is suggested, but Bleger nowhere mentions this and the concept is problematic, leaving open the question of how to listen to the silence of the setting. Bleger’s concept of encuadre can be applied to constants (invariants) in the wider world, the psychotic part of the personality being deposited in everything that is familiar and felt to be constant, including technology, which creates a “platform” for human activity.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Do we Know our Place? The Role of Psychoanalysis in Public Life

Elizabeth Allison

Elizabeth Allison

1-19 Torrington Place, London WC1E 7HB, UK
e.allison@ucl.ac.uk

London, UK

Elizabeth Allison  

Applied psychoanalysis, community, sociocultural context

The contributions to this Psychoanalytic Controversies section explore the question of what psychoanalysis may be able to contribute to thinking about some of the challenges currently confronting humanity and how such communications can be made effectively. This introduction to the section frames the debate with some reflections on anxieties that have been expressed about the application of psychoanalytic ideas beyond the clinical context, the risks of insularity, the need for appropriate humility, and the reality of the embeddedness of analytic practice, in particular social, cultural, and historical contexts. Contributions from Claudia Frank, Sudhir Kakar, Eli Zaretsky, Michael Rustin, Pratyusha Tummala-Narra, Magda Khouri, and Sally Weintrobe are introduced.

responseSet.title: On the Question of the Internal Frame
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Question of the Internal Frame

Howard Levine

Howard Levine

hblevinemd@gmail.com

Howard Levine  

Frame, setting, metapsychology, unstructured unconscious, distance analysis, telemetric treatments

This paper attempts to expand José Bleger's classic, metapsychological descriptions of the psychoanalytic frame to formulate and emphasize the role of the analyst's internal frame in establishing a psychoanalytic observational perspective in the analytic situation. The rationale for doing so follows from clinical necessity, especially when working with patients and psychic organizations that are ‘beyond neurosis' and in non-traditional settings such as distance and telemetric analyses. Clinically speaking, in its most effective state, the analyst’s internal frame can inform the possibility of an observational vertex aimed at the intuitive grasp of psychic reality rather than a sense-based, empirical observation of parameters denoted by the elements of a consensually validatable social reality.

responseSet.newSectionName: Celebrating the Work of Dana Birksted-Breen
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“We are Made of Time”: Reverberation and the Elasticity of Time in Psychoanalysis. Negotiating Different Temporalities in the Session**

Catalina Bronstein

Catalina Bronstein

112A Shirland Road, London W9 2BT, UK
c.bronstein@btinternet.com

Catalina Bronstein  

space, reverberation time, time elasticity, tridimensional space, fear of death, annihilation anxiety

Inspired by Dana Birksted-Breen's ideas on reverberation time, the author explores the changeability and transformation of the sensations of time and space and their connection to early embodied phantasies in the treatment of a 10-year-old boy. The experience of time changes (summarized under “time elasticity” to reflect the various forms this can take) is lived out in the transference relationship from the beginning of the therapeutic encounter. The author proposes the simultaneous development of the capacity to accept “objective” time, the establishment of a tri-dimensional space within the self and between objects and tolerating separateness and separation. The development of a capacity for symbolic thinking and depressive anxiety, as well as acceptance of the Oedipal situation and separation, has, as Dana Birksted-Breen underlined, a fundamental effect on the acceptance of objective time. This paper also discusses the difference between dealing with the difficulty of accepting objective time under the impact of fear of death and from the terror and nameless dread arising from the phantasy of the annihilation of time.

responseSet.title: Intuition and we-ness in Bion and Post-Bionian Field Theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intuition and we-ness in Bion and Post-Bionian Field Theory11

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

Piazza A. Botta, 1, Pavia 27100, Italy
gcivitarese@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-3466-4329

Giuseppe Civitarese  

Intuition, we-ness, reverie, analytic field, Bion, “ontology of we”

“Intuition” is probably the most frequently used term in all Bion’s writings. However, in order to understand its role in his thought it is essential to give it the clearest possible definition. The thesis of this article is that by intuition Bion means a “specific” psychoanalytic concept. It is thus possible to extract intuition from the vague and mystifying reading of it by some authors, whic runs the risk of falling into an empty “intuitionism”. For Bion, intuition is a psychoanalytic function of the analyst, the principal factors of which are the various expressions of dream-thought and insight. Furthermore, within the frame of the post-Bionian theory of the analytic field, the author suggests adding to these factors the use of the “we” vertex (or we-ness), i.e. to regard virtually every fact of analysis as co-created. The aim is to make the very concept of “field” more accessible. Compared with the metaphor of the analytic field, the concept of we-ness has both greater clinical versatility and greater pregnancy on the metapsychological plane. Indeed, it more directly reflects a radically social conception of human subjectivity: what is known in contemporary speculative thought – in J-L. Nancy, for example – as the “ontology of we”.

[[RunningHead]]

Enquiries into Virulent Identifications

Claudia Frank

Claudia Frank

cl.frank@t-online.de

Claudia Frank  

Identification, pathological supereog figure, Covid-19

Unquestioned vicious identifications with dubious (e.g. political) figures seem central in today's multiple crises. As in former dark times, when M. Klein described the dynamics triggered by an ‘inner Hitler', the approach offered in this contribution is to explore present forms, which we meet in the consulting room. As will be shown, a crucial step in this process lies in working through those challenges in the countertranserence.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Further Explorations into the Complexities of Femininity and Masculinity

Patrick Miller

Patrick Miller

93 Bd Saint-Michel, 75005 Paris, France
patmil75@gmail.com

Patrick Miller  

Penis-as-link, introjection, protransference

This text explores Dana Birksted-Breen's notions of penis-as-link and negative and positive femininity, rooted in primitive mental and bodily states, in a dialogue between the manifestations of the biological body and their interpretation by the psyche in the construction of a sexed bodily mind. When the capacities for introjection have been impaired from an early stage, there is a great difficulty in representing and relating to a receptive internal space, hence to one's femininity, be it for a girl or a boy. This capacity for introjection in its relation to femininity plays a great part in the analytic capacity to display one's bodily receiver instrument as a sounding board for the analysand's unconscious. Instead of countertransference, one might think in terms of protransference.

responseSet.title: Editorial: IJP News for a New Year
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial: IJP News for a New Year

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

fjrgrier@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-852X

Francis Grier  

As the IJP embarks on a new year, I would like to start by thanking the editors, reviewers and readers for making the Journal what it is – a vehicle for opening up truly international conversations about psychoanalysis today. In the last year, these conversations took place not only through the pages of the Journal, but via our International Zoom Study Meetings, with eminent analysts coming together online to think about and discuss the major issues facing psychoanalysis at present. Richard Zimmer has recently presented his paper “Post-Termination Self-Analysis and the Relinquishment of the Psychoanalytic Frame: Thoughts on a Fragment of Self-Analytic Work following a Traumatic Termination” (Zimmer 2021), discussed by Fakhry Davids and Clara Nemas, and on 8 June John Steiner will present his paper “Mourning in Hamlet: Turning Ancestral Ghosts into Ancestors” (https://www.theijp.org/ijp-zoom-study-meetings/). The discussants will be Bruce Reis and Rachel Blass.

Through the now widespread use of Zoom, the Editors and Board Members of the Journal are now able to meet more regularly than has been historically possible. In December the IJP Board participated in our annual IJP Zoom Board Meeting, with high attendance and some very fruitful discussion. In January this year, we launched our first IJP Conference, celebrating the work of the former longstanding Editor-in-Chief, Dana Birksted-Breen. It was a marvellous event, and I would like to thank our contributors and all those who participated in making it such a rich and invigorating day. While meeting online has opened up great opportunities, meeting in person remains central to our collaborations, and so this year I am very pleased to be meeting with colleagues in New York and Florence.

Where these meetings, events and conferences have enabled us to come together as a community of readers, I am delighted that this community has grown dramatically over the last year. Now, at the start of 2024, our subscriber base for members of psychoanalytic societies (“IJP block subscribers”) is over 6000, whereas three years ago it was around 2000. This major growth has been thanks to our new initiative of making the Journal available to block subscribers at a more affordable price, while promoting migration from print to online usage. With this increase in access to the Journal, more readers are now able to engage with – and hopefully sometimes write about – contemporary thought in the here and now.

responseSet.title: Inhibition
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Inhibition**

Solange Carton

Solange Carton

Montpellier, France
so.carton@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-3466-4329

Solange Carton  

Inhibition, depression, Eros and the death drive, energetic point of view

This article explores the notion of inhibition at a theoretical and clinical level in psychoanalysis. The first part follows the development of the notion in Freud’s work, from the “Project” (1950a [1895]) to Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety (1926d). It identifies the two approaches to inhibition, the first from an energetic point of view, the second from the angle of its relations to anxiety. The second part of the article is devoted to the links between inhibition and Freud’s thoughts about death, in particular in Inhibitions, Symptoms and Anxiety and the links to the death drive. It draws on some of Jones’ notes and presents a brief clinical illustration. The third part focuses more particularly on general inhibition, especially in depression and melancholia. Based on the treatment of one patient, the author shows how the slow process of overcoming general inhibition is achieved through the gradual use of negation. From an economic point of view, it is suggested that psychoanalytic treatment, through the transference and associative speech, has an effect on the depletion of energy by diverting the “suction” of stimuli, paving the way for the formation of drive representatives, or inhibitions as symptoms, which will need to be dissected.

[[RunningHead]]

Adjusting the Distance

Michael Feldman

Michael Feldman

London, UK
mmf@mmfeldman.co.uk

Michael Feldman  

Distance, claustro, agoraphobia

This paper describes the anxiety evoked in a patient threatened by invasion or engulfment by his object on the one hand, and the fears of isolation and abandonment on the other. The author illustrates the patient's strugles to find a distance between himself and his object he can tolerate. The analyst has also to cope with the anxieties evoked by the patient's projections, and find a distance between himself and his patient that enables him to think and work.

responseSet.title: Introduction of A candidate at Work: The case of Ms. N.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction of A candidate at Work: The case of Ms. N.

Francis Grier

Francis Grier

fjrgrier@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3757-852X

Francis Grier  

For some time Abbot Bronstein, who in his role as editor of the Analyst at Work section in the International Journal has so successfully sought out presenters from many and varied different clinical situations, has had the idea that it would be good if we could present an analysis together with analytical responses from a psychoanalytic training situation. Naturally I have supported this aim from the very start. On the one hand, candidates by definition have a lot to learn in our field, theoretically and clinically, but on the other hand, it is not only that many candidates already have crucial experiences of life which they bring to their training and to their training cases, but also that they will often bring qualities of imagination and insight that may arise precisely because they are not yet saturated in a psychoanalytic atmosphere. Many a supervisor will often find that they are not just teaching psychoanalysis to the candidate, they are also learning psychoanalysis from the candidate.

Human nature being what it is, it is rare that one encounters tidy splits between the supervisor who knows and the candidate who doesn’t, or even between a supervisor who knows they don’t know and a candidate with unexpected imaginative insight. Usually there is a mess, not quite chaos, but those moments when a candidate comes up with an unexpected insight are not necessarily obvious. They may take the form more of an intuitive gesturing towards a different possibility, which the supervisor may or may not realize at the time contains the glimmer of new insight. Meanwhile, perhaps the supervisor may be trying to impart to the candidate the importance of many subtle kinds of boundaries, perhaps in how they speak and formulate interpretations, which task the supervisor may manage with more or less precision, whilst the candidate may have to work through quite a difficult period of feeling overly controlled.

responseSet.title: A Candidate at Work: The Case of Ms. N
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Candidate at Work: The Case of Ms. N

Charles Baekeland

Charles Baekeland

cebaekeland@hotmail.com

Charles Baekeland  

Introduction

Ms. N., in her late twenties, first consulted in February 2017. She had found my name through the American Embassy in Madrid since she was looking for an English-speaking psychoanalyst. When she came in for the first interview, she took on an amused, ironic look as if there were something ridiculous or funny about the situation, or about me, and that we both knew it. She was consulting because she would vomit after having sex.

She described herself as engaging in predatory sexual behavior, compulsively seeking out men to have sex with and then, inevitably, vomiting. Once she had seduced the men, she would see her friends and tell great stories of her conquests, making them laugh. In her telling of this, there were repeated references to eating or devouring men; when I linked it to the vomiting, she saw the connection, and quickly understood my tentative interpretations.

Clever, witty, and cultured, Ms. N. grew up in the USA, and worked in Latin America. She had a knowing, worldly, cynical attitude of someone who’s seen it all and isn’t surprised by anything, and tried to maneuver me into a complicity of just how thoroughly absurd a psychoanalytic interview was. At the end of the first interview, she guffawed and said that if she had had to cast someone as a psychoanalyst, she would have cast me exactly as I was dressed, and in my consulting room.

During the initial three interviews she mentioned that her grandfather had touched her when she was nine, without specifying how, and that her father was violent towards her mother. He was an alcoholic and lost his job; the whole family had to be quiet during the day when he was sleeping on the sofa. He swung from being the life of the party, laughing and playing the guitar, to being a raging fury and “strangling her brother”1 when she was seven. She talked of growing up in a violent, sexualized2 and dangerous neighborhood where she learned to be tough. The descriptions of these incidents and their context were permeated by an exhibitionistic bravado.

responseSet.title: Discussion of A Candidate at Work: The Case of Ms. N.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of A Candidate at Work: The Case of Ms. N.

Vic Sedlak

Vic Sedlak

sedlak@blueyonder.co.uk

Vic Sedlak  

I would like to begin by thanking Charles for offering us his material in such an open and accessible way.

While the work that Charles reports was undertaken as part of his training and hence can offer an insight into how a candidate develops his or her clinical acumen, I want to focus on the ways in which this material allows us to focus on the lifelong task we have as analysts. To my mind this is the task of expanding our emotional repertoire as we encounter different patients throughout our professional lives. When I was training in the British Psychoanalytical Society Nina Coltart told us in a seminar on clinical technique that it would take us ten years after qualification to feel assured in our identity as psychoanalysts – my experience is that it is something we strive towards until the day we retire.

While we work to alleviate our patients’ suffering, I also maintain the view, perhaps paradoxically, that the aim of psychoanalytic treatment is to enable the patient to suffer their emotions – to be able to feel at an emotional level disappointment, or sadness or jealousy – to be able to suffer emotional pain. And this links with what I have indicated as being fundamental to the analyst’s developmental task – that is to say, experiencing and suffering the painful challenges that they encounter in the work.

If a patient is unable to suffer emotionally the pain that life’s events throw at them, they will find various other means of dealing with the pain. To turn to the patient Charles presents: she somatizes pain – she vomits after her sexual conquests rather than experiencing the presumably uncontainable feelings consequent on her sexual experiences; soon we hear that she suffers from diarrhea after seeing her analyst in the street with another woman. Another means of trying to avoid suffering is to minimize it – she does this with her belittling attitude to needing treatment – inviting Charles to collude with her dismissal of her need for analysis, which he manfully resists. Anna Freud (1972) wrote of a particular defense against suffering emotional pain – you can identify with the aggressor, and this I think we can see in what I will call Ms. N.’s phallic identification (of which more later) – she is the sexual predator who then boasts of her conquests. Although she speaks of a warm and caring mother, which might imply her awareness of an inner child that needs such care, this does not survive her encounter with emotional pain – it becomes submissive, I will argue, to her identification with her father. When Charles interprets that she would like to be looked after by him as by a mother, she remembers a mother who laughed at her when she was in pain. So we are left in doubt about whether she had the experience of being sufficiently cared for; but little doubt about whether she can tolerate the pain of feeling like a baby who needs a mother.

Maxine Anderson  

Jonathan Lear has written an important book for our times, one that emerges from the recent pandemic with questions about how we might maintain, even strengthen, our capacities to be human and resilient when faced with potential catastrophe. And yet the book also appears to continue some of the major themes from Lear’s earlier book, Radical Hope (2006), suggesting that he has been considering for decades issues of maintaining one’s humanity amidst the pressures of personal and cultural upheaval.

responseSet.title: Gratitude, Freedom and Refusal
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gratitude, Freedom and Refusal

Jonathan Lear

Jonathan Lear

1130 E 59th Street, Chicago, IL 60637, USA
jlear@uchicago.edu

Jonathan Lear  

Affects/affective disorders, contemporary Kleinians, ethics, Freudian theory, history of psychoanalysis, philosophy

This paper is an exploration of gratitude as a fundamental concept in psychoanalysis. Melanie Klein’s classic article “Envy and Gratitude” (1957) named gratitude at one pole on an axis of human suffering and flourishing, but with a few notable exceptions, the article stimulated research into envy. This paper explores the historical and philosophical traditions that have, to some extent unconsciously, influenced our contemporary understandings of gratitude. The paper also works to explore the social and ethical meanings of gratitude as well as gratitude’s psychoanalytic significance. The aim is to uncover the overall psychic significance of gratitude and its place in human flourishing.

Facing the Unthinkable: Life-Threatening Illness in the Analyst and its Impact on the Analytic Couple**

Maxine Nelson

Maxine Nelson

Seattle, Washington, USA
Maxinenelson1@gmail.com

Maxine Nelson  

Terminal, illness, analyst

This article explores the impact of the analyst’s life-threatening illness on the analytic couple; it is informed through two theoretical lenses - Freud’s ideas about the vicissitudes of mourning, which have been elaborated on by Melanie Klein and John Steiner, and Christopher Bollas’s ideas about destiny and idiom. Clinical material will focus on my on-going work with a middle-aged man who has a history of early abandonment and loss and who struggles with being able to remain separate from his objects while being in relationship with them.

responseSet.title: Freud’s Interpretation in “Medusa’s Head” and Some Alternative Psychoanalytic Implications of Ovid’s Medusa
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud’s Interpretation in “Medusa’s Head” and Some Alternative Psychoanalytic Implications of Ovid’s Medusa

B. J. Sokol

B. J. Sokol

London, UK
b.sokol@zen.co.uk

London, UK

B. J. Sokol  

Medusa, Ovid, Freud

Freud’s very brief 1922 paper on the beheading of Medusa by Perseus wisely concludes with a call for a further examination of the sources of the legend. A now widespread interpretation of this legend is based (often without acknowledgement) on an addition to traditions concerning Medusa made in Ovid’s Metamorphoses. It is argued here that this Ovidian innovation has often been misinterpreted, and that a more careful reading of Metamorphoses supports neither a widely alleged exclusively vengeful portrayal of Medusa, nor Freud’s portrayal of Medusa’s decapitation as solely a pitiable and terrible symbol of castration. Instead, Ovid’s complex treatments of myths involving Medusa, Minerva and Perseus present parallels with Kleinian insights into phantasy attacks on fecundity, and into imagined revivals of dead or damaged inside babies. Thus the “displacement upwards” of the fearful castrated maternal genital envisioned in Freud’s “Medusa’s Head” must stand beside a quite different “displacement upwards” of the life-giving maternal genital. Indeed, tradition holds that Medusa’s beheading gives rise to the birth of vigorous twins. Together with allied details, this aligns Ovid’s masterwork with theories that modify or displace the so-called “sexual phallic monism” that some believe taints Freud’s theories of gender development.

Ellen Sparer  

“Complete candour coupled with the strictest discretion.”

This “pact” between analysand and analyst constitutes the “analytic situation.” (Freud 1940 {1938}, 173)

This Education Section, the first in a two-part series, aims to explore the sense given to the psychoanalytic situation and its borders, both physical and psychic, as well as the signification of its different terminologies - frame, setting, site, situation - terms that have varied from author to author, region to region, according to the meaning ascribed to that specific space which makes psychoanalysis possible.

The frame or setting could thus be said to be a “pact” - that is, an agreement between patient and analyst regarding the conditions within which the analytic work will be undertaken. In a curious coincidence, during the mid-twentieth century, authors from each of the three regions wrote what would become key papers or concepts regarding the analytic protagonists and the significance of the space they occupied together during an analysis.

Jose Bleger’s (1967) paper ‘The Psychoanalysis of the Psycho-analytic Frame’, Donald W. Winnicott’s (1954, 285-286) remarks on the psychoanalytic setting, and Leo Stone’s (1961) The Psychoanalytic Situation: An Examination of Its Development and Essential Nature would each raise and reveal questions regarding the nature of and the forces inherent in the setting itself within the analytic process. During this same period in France, Lacan’s unorthodox use of “scansion” - that is, abbreviating session times - brought about a flowering of reflection on the meaning and the function of the setting/frame.

Twenty-five years prior to his condensed remarks on the analytic pact, Freud (1911-1915 {1914}, 127, 133), had noted the need for regular session times, fixed fees, etc. While he acknowledged that the supine position was a remnant of the hypnosis technique, he added with his well-known honesty that he couldn’t “put up with being stared at by other people eight hours a day” … (134). Said otherwise, it was being in the patient’s perceptual field, wherein seeing the analyst’s facial expressions risked unduly influencing the patient’s associativity, that contributed to Freud’s proposing the now habitual arrangement of couch-chair.

responseSet.title: Frame, Setting, Process, and Movement
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Frame, Setting, Process, and Movement

José Calich

José Calich

Porto Alegre, Brazil
jccalich@sppa.org.br

José Calich  

Frame, setting, psychoanalytic process

The author addresses the theme of frame and setting in psychoanalysis, suggesting that they are “functions” of the psychoanalytic process. Frame is defined as the external components of the context that enable the development of a process, and setting as the primarily psychoanalytic elements within the analyst’s mind, necessary for establishing an analysing situation where a process should take place. The author emphasizes that the characteristics of both also define the outline of the process, while discussing attributes that would aid in its development. The author proposes the creation of an imaginary model regarding possible invariants in psychoanalytic theories and theories of technique with the intention of deepening the understanding of the relationship between frame, setting and process. The author concludes with a reflection on the effect of implicit theories on the construction of frame and setting.

Film Review Essay: Inside Llewyn Davis: Faltering Steps in the Incredible Journey from Adolescence to Adulthood

Noel Hess

Noel Hess

Flat 4 Mullion Court, 112 Finchley Rd London NW3 5JH
UK
nchess24@gmail.com

Noel Hess  

Adolescence, development, mourning

The paper discusses the Coen brothers’ film Inside Llewyn Davis as representing in microcosm the developmental trajectory from late adolescence towards adulthood for the title character. This entails negotiating the problems of unconscious guilt, failure to mourn and an awareness of time in order to effect a movement from masochistic melancholy and hidden grandiosity to being able to relinquish omnipotent phantasies, say au revoir to internal persecutors and achieve a degree of self-possession.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Ecology: The Unconscious and the Environment by Cosimo Schinaia, New York, Routledge Taylor and Francis Group, 2022, 168 pp., $34.36, ISBN 978-1-032-11482-8

Lindsay Clarkson

Lindsay Clarkson

llcmd@earthlink.net

Lindsay Clarkson  

The inferno of the living is not something that will be: if there is one, it is what is already here, the inferno where we live every day, that we form by being together. There are two ways to escape suffering it. The first is easy for many: accept the inferno and become such a part of it that you can no longer see it. The second is risky and demands constant vigilance and apprehension: seek and learn to recognize who and what, in the midst of the inferno, are not inferno, then make them endure, give them space. (Italo Calvino, Invisible Cities)

Cosimo Schinaia’s vision in Psychoanalysis and Ecology is surely in the category of those who seek to imagine and create a new space and way of being in response to the ecological disruption caused by our actions. To recognize and face up to the hard facts of the environmental devastation that is unfolding, while maintaining a balanced outlook to enable thought and conceive of reparative action takes a great deal of internal work. It is evident that Cosimo Schinaia, a training and supervising analyst at the Italian Society of Psychoanalysis, who practises in Genoa, has devoted serious attention to his topic.

The book under review is a natural outgrowth of the enriching dialogue between diverse disciplines, an interweaving of external and internal worlds, that Schinaia (2016) addressed in a previous book, Psychoanalysis and Architecture, The Inside and the Outside. In the current volume, Schinaia expands the definition of “external” to include the natural world. Eschewing the phrase “applied psychoanalysis”, Schinaia instead suggests “involved psychoanalysis”, a reframing that is integrative and inclusive. The positive connotation of involvement can counterbalance our tendencies to retreat to inner concerns as a defence against unbearable affect, and instead engage with the evolving crisis.

Psychoanalysis and Ecology is short and well referenced; it seeks to connect two extensive bodies of knowledge and approaches to the world. The ten chapters read as self-contained essays, for example “Human Beings and the Environment”, “Freud and the Environment”, “Work-Health Balance Conflict” and “Servants of the Future”. Schinaia’s myriad sources reveal his wide-ranging curiosity and include psychoanalytic literature, blogs on climate, fiction from Dante to Calvino, philosophy and poetry. There is less emphasis on ecology. Psychoanalysts will be more familiar with citations that address the inner weather and interior landscapes of clinical concern. Recently, psychoanalysis has grown more open to the relevance for personality development of an ecological perspective that addresses the relationship between living organisms and the physical and organismic environment in which they grow and reproduce. Our embeddedness within the natural world is a fact of life in Money-Kyrle’s term, and this reality has an emotional meaning to us that affects our treatment of environmental concerns (Money-Kyrle 1971).

Creative Repetition and Intersubjectivity: Contemporary Freudian Explorations of Trauma, Memory and Clinical Process by Bruce Reis, London and New York, Routledge, 2020, 120pp., £34.99, ISBN 978-0-367-26120-7

Josh Cohen

Josh Cohen

j.cohen@gold.ac.uk

Josh Cohen  

That five of the nine chapters in this book comprise previously published articles (three of them in the IJP), written over the course of a decade and encompassing a range of clinical and theoretical themes, might give the impression that it is a collection of papers. While this is not strictly wrong, it misses its remarkable consistency of vision and sensibility, the intricate articulation of component parts that make it very much a book.

Reis’s book is hospitable to a richly diverse range of analytic traditions - Anglophone and French especially, but also South American writers, all interests which build on his deep grounding in Freud. But as Christopher Bollas observes in a preface as brief as it is exact, Reis mercifully avoids the dutifully plodding “review of the literature”. While his psychoanalytic erudition is beyond doubt, the links he makes across different lines of thought are more associative than exhaustive, an “inspiring salon” (Bollas’s phrase) rather than a long and static register of names.

Reis’s prose is stately, careful, discrete. The book is notable for its engagement with academic as well as clinical psychoanalytic literature (two bodies of writing that speak to one another too rarely) and his formal diction can call to mind, at least at first, the signature style of the academic humanities. But this high style also facilitates the emergence, at key points, and especially in the clinical discussions, of a more distinctly personal voice.

Perhaps the most memorable instance here is a remark regarding his countertransference response to Sylvie, a perfumier (or “nose”) and drinks consultant, whom he discusses in his final chapter: “It may help to know that cocktails are on the short list of things that delight me" (108). That one modest adjective “short”, at once world-weary (reading it, I had my own 268

responseSet.title: War, Terror and Mourning. Cultural Memory in the Inner Dialogue
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

War, Terror and Mourning. Cultural Memory in the Inner Dialogue

Georg Bruns

Georg Bruns

Berlin, Germany
gj.bruns@t-online.de
http://orcid.org/0009-0003-2355-2609

Georg Bruns  

mental effects of war and terror, unconscious in the community, cultural memory, collective memory, myths, mourning

This text compares four essays dealing with war, state terror in dictatorships, social violence such as racism, mourning and the avoidance of mourning. It shows that dictatorships share similarities in their techniques of manipulation, linguistic style and reference to history. They seek to exploit national myths through manipulative alienation. Myths are a central element of cultural memory, and their effect can be understood through a model of internal dialogue. This dialogue determines whether the regime's attempts at manipulation are successful.

responseSet.title: The Inability to Mourn and Nationalism in Japan after 1945
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Inability to Mourn and Nationalism in Japan after 1945

Kai Ogimoto and Tomas Plaenkers

Tomas Plaenkers

New York, NY, USA
kai.ogimoto.0202@gmail.com

Frankfurt a. M., Land Hessen, Germany

Tomas Plaenkers  

Failure to deal with the issue of collective and social loss increases the risk of extreme nationalism. When taken too far, a repetition of manic defence can arise that manifests itself in the form of war. In this paper, the notion of the “inability to mourn” by the German Psychoanalysts A. and M. Mitshcerlich (1967) is discussed in relation to the problem of Japan’s post World War II nationalism, and its silence on social matters. The process of confronting past atrocities committed by the state is then discussed from the perspective of structural theory.

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis and Politics
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Politics

Eli Zaretsky

Eli Zaretsky

New York, NY, USA
elizaretsky@earthlink.net

Eli Zaretsky  

Politics, radicalism, leftism

Psychoanalysis and Politics argue that psychoanalysis is at its root a social or group theory, to which a theory of individual psychology is integral. This formulation follows from Freud's Group Psychology, which defines individual psychology as a derivative of group psychology, “still incomplete.” The article historicizes the analytic conception of the individual in terms of the authors' conception of personalize, spelled out in Secrets of the Soul. Three versions of psychoanalytic social theory are discussed: Freudo-Marxism, the New Left and feminism and the “relational turn.”

responseSet.title: Obituary for Irma Brennan Pick
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary for Irma Brennan Pick

Fakhry Davids

Fakhry Davids

fakhrydavids@gmail.com

Fakhry Davids  

Irma Brennan Pick, the widely respected British psychoanalyst, has died at the age of 89. Originally from South Africa, she travelled to the UK in 1955, aged 21, with her new husband to begin his training as a psychoanalyst in London. Irma herself first entered the training in child psychotherapy at the Tavistock Clinic in London, later going on to do the analytic training at the Institute of Psychoanalysis herself. These were years of consolidation of the three psychoanalytic traditions that were to co-exist within the British Society under the Gentlemen’s Agreement that settled the Controversial Discussions - today’s Kleinians, Contemporary Freudians and Independents (King and Steiner 1991). Outside the Society, the Hampstead Clinic was the professional hub of Anna Freud, from which important clarifications of the Freudian position were to flow. The Children’s Department of the Tavistock Clinic, which would eventually move literally into the next street, was heavily Kleinian in orientation and a hive of clinical and theoretical creativity. Irma Pick thrived in this atmosphere. In her two trainings she was taught and supervised by some of the leading figures in Kleinian psychoanalysis at the time, including Hanna Segal, Betty Joseph, Esther Bick, Herbert Rosenfeld and Wilfred Bion. Her analyst was Hans Thorner.

Irma Lief was born in Arcadia, a suburb of Pretoria, South Africa, on 13 April 1934, the daughter of Min (née Jacobs) and Joe, both emigrants from Latvia who had arrived in South Africa around the turn of the twentieth century. The couple married towards the end of the First World War, and Irma had two older siblings, a sister and a brother; two of her maternal aunts also lived with the family. Her father, who died when she was 13, was a chronic asthmatic. He struggled in business but her mother set up a successful secretarial college in the heart of Pretoria. Although their painful history, as exiles from the Baltic region, was so recent, her parents said little of the stories that lay behind this, something that Irma partially rectified in 2018 when she used the opportunity of a teaching trip to Latvia to compensate for her lack of curiosity about these matters as a child. Painful as 427

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Surviving Object: Psychoanalytic Clinical Essays on Psychic Survival-of-the Object, by Jan Abram, London and New York, Routledge, 2022, 210 p., £31.99, ISBN: 978-1-03-207518-1

Marie Lenormand

Marie Lenormand

Aix-en-Provence, France
marie.lenormand@univ-amu.fr

Marie Lenormand  

Two Images

Two images at the very outset of Jan Abram’s book The Surviving Object set the scene for her argument. The Ukiyo-e Kindergarten - Carp Fish painted by Chikanobu is a cheerful portrait of a mother holding a child with her left arm while playing with a toy fish suspended to a bamboo stick with her right hand. On the opposite page, Magritte’s The Spirit of Geometry (Motherhood) is more unsettling. In this paradoxical scene, the proportions and roles are inverted, showing a chimeric adult mother with a baby’s head holding a “baby” with an adult woman’s head. The tender gestures are obfuscated by the sad atmosphere emanating from the rigid and inexpressive faces of the adult baby and the inaccessible mother with an infant’s body. According to Abram, the Ukiyo-e shows the surviving object leading to self and creativity. On the other hand, Magritte’s painting represents what she calls the non-surviving object.

An Ariadne Thread: “The Psychic Survival-of-the-Object”

At the very start, Abram tells us that “the psychic survival-of-the-object” served as her “vital Ariadne thread” (1) for her “becoming and being a psychoanalyst”. In coining this term (Winnicott himself does not make use of it), she wants to designate, for the very first time, “the core concept in Winnicott’s theoretical matrix” and its “fundamental role”. According to her definition, the survival-of-the-object

refers to the crucial processes involved between object and subject that initiate healthy psychic development. Because it is only due to the object’s psychic survival of the subject’s primitive benign aggression, that the subject will be able to place the object outside in the world. (1)

This formula is thus at the core of both Winnicott’s lifelong quest and Abram’s borrowings from him in her own psychoanalytic practice. It serves as a bridge between two sides of her work: her re-reading of Winnicott’s oeuvre and her own Winnicott-inspired analytic practice.

Before addressing these two points, let us note that putting the notion of the object’s survival - or the failure thereof - at the centre is a way for Abram to stress that the hypothesis of the “vicissitudes of the parent-infant relationship” has “radical” consequences “for psychoanalysis - in practice and in theory” (148). By shifting the emphasis from psychosexual development to the role of the environment, Winnicott introduced a “r/evolution in psychoanalysis” (xxiii). An “essential hallmark”, according to Abram, of Winnicott's thinking, this shift constitutes his “contribution to an understanding of madness”. More specifically, the introduction of the concept of “psychic survival-of-the-object”, encompassing the “destructive element in the crude primitive excited idea” (1), casts a new light on Freud’s death drive.

responseSet.title: Mourning, Melancholia and Machines: An Applied Psychoanalytic Investigation of Mourning in the Age of Griefbots
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mourning, Melancholia and Machines: An Applied Psychoanalytic Investigation of Mourning in the Age of Griefbots**

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

London, UK
alemma@mac.com

London, UK

Alessandra Lemma  

Ethics, technology, “grief tech”, time, melancholia, mourning, griefbot

Death and mourning are being shaped by posthumous opportunities for the dead to affect current life in ways not possible in pre-digital generations. The psychological and sociological impact of the dead ‘online’ and of ‘grief tech’ is only beginning to be understood. It has not yet been explored psychoanalytically until this paper that examines one type of grief tech, namely the griefbot. This development is critically explored through a psychoanalytic reading of an episode of Black Mirror. I suggest that a psychoanalytic model of mourning provides an invaluable perspective to help us to think about this technology’s potential as well as the psychological and ethical risks it poses. I argue that the immortalisation of the dead through digital permanence works against facing the painful reality of loss and the recognition of otherness, which is fundamental to psychic growth and to the integrity of our relationships with others. Drawing on Derrida’s conceptualization of ‘originary mourning’, I suggest that mourning is an interminable process that challenges us to preserve within the self the otherness of the lost object. The tools we use for mourning need to be assessed first and foremost against this psychological and fundamentally ethical process.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to “The Older Analyst at Work: The Old Man and the Sea?”

Abbot A. Bronstein and Cynthia Ellis Gray

Cynthia Ellis Gray

abbot.bronstein@gmail.com

Cynthia Ellis Gray  

This Analyst at Work section examines the work of an older psychoanalyst as he ages yet continues to work as a psychoanalyst. Eike Hinze describes his work with a disturbed young man. Decisions about starting an analysis and the struggles involved in ‘reaching’ this patient form part of the question of whether this is a ‘quest’, or another analysis, near the end of an analytic career.

Of particular note is Dr. Hinze’s explicit use of his own ‘reverie’/ countertransference/ unconscious states to form interventions. The two discussants express their own understanding of how they might approach the problems posed. They both speak to the forms of intervening that differ from their own, and their clinical understanding of revealing one's own associations in the clinical hour. They also speak to how the process of aging might have influenced the clinical work itself.

The possible shift in technique over the course of Dr. Hinze's clinical career is more difficult to assess: does it come from age, maturity, shifts in theory and technique, or an intense desire to make emotional contact within this particular patient and clinical setting? These are the “question marks” conveyed in their discussions, like the one in the title itself.

responseSet.title: Discussion of “The Older Analyst at Work: The Old Man and the Sea?”
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of “The Older Analyst at Work: The Old Man and the Sea?”

Anne Amos

Anne Amos

anneamos56@gmail.com

Anne Amos  

Eike Hinze describes his work with Mr A in order for us to consider what, if any, may be the significance of being an older analyst. The material Eike gives us comes from a time when the analyst is aged 83 and his patient is 46. I will begin with a summary of my understanding of this clinical material and then discuss the significance of the lack of “recognition of the inevitability of time and ultimately death” (Money-Kyrle 1971, 443) in this particular analysis, which I suggest lies in the patient and is projected into his analyst. The analyst’s material age is not irrelevant, but I find the part it has played in this analysis and the use to which this patient puts it interesting.

Discussion of the clinical account

On reading this clinical account I am first struck by two powerful images from the interface of the human and animal worlds, both at the start of Eike’s account and at the start of Mr A’s life. Eike links himself to the old fisherman, Santiago, depicted in Ernest Hemingway’s last novel The Old Man and the Sea, who struggled to capture a marlin and then the precious fish is destroyed by sharks as Santiago is not able to bring the huge marlin safely to shore. Mr A describes his mother lifting a cat, which I take to represent Mr A as a baby, looking as if she were touching “a dead and disgusting object, without any empathy or warmth”. These images from the natural world evoke a fisherman and a mother unable to hold a creature who is different from them, too big or too repugnant. I think this is Mr A’s unconscious phantasy – he is too much for his object, as if he is a different species from those trying to look after him and one with whom they cannot cope. It is this underlying belief about himself that he enacts in his life and in his analysis.

Eike’s account conveys that he feels troubled about his strange patient who he thinks will “get the upper hand” and I think we too, as readers, share this. Mr A talks little in the sessions and seems emotionally frozen, leading his analyst to be more active than he would normally be. Overall, there is a sense of a hard-to-reach patient, for whom it is very difficult to feel empathy. His analyst, on the other hand, is working hard, bringing in his own thoughts and associations in lieu, I would suggest, of the patient’s. The atmosphere conveyed is in stark contrast to Eike’s other treatment which he describes at the end of his account. In his analysis of Mr B, we are immediately struck by the touching resonance between patient and analyst leading to fruitful and moving work. This could not be more different to Mr A’s analysis.

responseSet.title: Finding a Hospitable Home – Transitioning as a Last Resort
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Finding a Hospitable Home – Transitioning as a Last Resort**

Jules Schaper

Jules Schaper

jules.schaper@gmail.com

Jules Schaper  

While reading an article from Professor Alessandra Lemma – a clinical psychologist and psychoanalyst with much experience in the field of transgender care – I was struck by the term “hospitable home” and felt called to write something about the subject from my own experience.

The term resonated so much because I found it so true. I have always felt like I was looking for a “hospitable home”. A body I could feel at home in, and also a safe house around it as a second shell of protection. And I’ve always felt that, somehow, my doubts about gender had to do with that. That it was a search for (a safe) space, space to breathe, for room to move, and that I would only be able to achieve that in another body.

I transitioned more than two decades ago, and when I visited the gender team at the time, my story fell into the category of “primary transsexuality”, meaning I had felt I was a boy from a very young age. After a standard number of interviews, I was given “green light” without much ado. But now, after all these years, I still cannot say that gender is no longer an “issue” for me, or that I have found my home. The quest lingers on and I believe I’ll only find some peace in trying to make sense of the source of my discomfort. Unlike people who see gender dysphoria as their end diagnosis and transitioning as the solution to their problems (a very tempting idea) I’ve always seen it as a consequence of a greater discomfort, as part of a web of intricately linked causes of suffering.

My medical transition was rough and left me with many complications. By medically transitioning, in the search for a body that is a hospitable home, there’s a chance you could find yourself in an even less hospitable home; a body damaged by surgery, a body forever at the mercy of synthetic hormones, a body that is growing older in ways that are unexpected or undesired, a body that may become just as foreign and unsafe as the one before had been. It is true that I find it comfortable to be looked upon as a man, that it suits me somehow, but the price has simply been too high.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to: “Finding a Hospitable Home – Transitioning as a Last Resort” by Jules Schaper

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

dr.alessandra.lemma@gmail.com

Alessandra Lemma  

Dear Editor,

It is a rare privilege to receive a response to a paper I have written from someone with lived experience of what I am writing about. Jules Schaper’s generous, courageous and moving “free association” to a term I used in my paper about transgender – “a hospitable home” – provides us with rich food for thought whilst the ongoing debates about transgender continue to divide clinicians and the transgender community alike.

I found Jules Schaper’s account deeply affecting and moving, and it resonated with the struggles articulated by some of my transgender patients. Specifically, I returned to this sentence:

After all these years, I still cannot say that gender is no longer an “issue” for me, or that I have found my home. The quest lingers on, and I believe I’ll only find some peace in trying to make sense of the source of my discomfort. Unlike people who see gender dysphoria as their end diagnosis and transitioning as the solution to their problems (a very tempting idea) I’ve always seen it as a consequence of a greater discomfort, as part of a web of intricately linked causes of suffering. (my italics)

The “web of intricately linked causes of suffering” poignantly captures the experiences my transgender patients have shared with me over the years, irrespective of whether they then chose to medically transition or not. However, in the context of gender-affirmative care, once gender issues are raised, children and young people’s wider holistic needs can be neglected, with a narrow focus on their gender-related distress. Jules Schaper invites us instead to consider the importance of psychological support that addresses a broader range of problems to reduce the risk of diagnostic overshadowing.

The debates about the merits and dangers of a gender-affirming approach continue, but what is increasingly clear is that the distress and confusion that we encounter clinically often conceals a “web of intricately linked causes of suffering” that is not clarified or resolved in any straightforward manner, if at all, through medical transitioning. Even when transitioning offers something of value to the individual – after all, Jules Schaper shares that, “It is true that I find it comfortable to be looked upon as a man, that it suits me somehow” – he suggests that we cannot overlook that its psychic and physical “costs” are sometimes just “too high”, and that medical transitioning perhaps only ought to be a “last resort”.

responseSet.title: “Some Remarks on the Unconscious” Freud’s Lost 1922 Lecture as a Messenger of Far-Reaching Changes
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

“Some Remarks on the Unconscious” Freud’s Lost 1922 Lecture as a Messenger of Far-Reaching Changes

Patrizia Giampieri-Deutsch

Patrizia Giampieri-Deutsch

Patrizia.Giampieri-Deutsch@univie.ac.at

Patrizia Giampieri-Deutsch  

A Brief Precursor of The Ego and the Id

Freud’s seminal, however – regrettably – no longer available, lecture “Some Remarks on the Unconscious” at the 7th International Psycho-Analytical Congress in Berlin in the year 1922 should be considered as a forerunner of Freud’s later substantial development toward the introduction of a structural frame of reference in The Ego and the Id (Freud 1923b, SE XIX, 12–66/RSE XIX, 10–58). For this reason, the brief but still preserved synopsis (Freud 1922f, SE XIX, 3–4/RSE XIX, 4) of Freud’s lecture deserves a commentary by way of an introduction.1

Freud developed his conception of the mind, which he termed “mental apparatus”, progressively. Although Freud himself did not bequeath a systematic theory of the mind, his theoretical assumptions in this regard could – due to their striking coherence – subsequently be subdivided into phases by David Rapaport and further specified by Joseph Sandler, Christopher Dare, and Alex Holder by identifying three phases and naming then Freud’s models of the mind, even though Freud had not formulated them as such (Rapaport 1959; Sandler, Dare, and Holder 1972a, 1972b, 1972c, and 1997; cf. Giampieri-Deutsch 2012, 2020).

According to this useful partition, in a first, still pre-analytical phase from around 1886 to 1897, Freud worked within the frame of reference of an affect-trauma model of the psychological mental apparatus or mind.

In a second phase from 1897 to 1923, Freud introduced a “topographical”, i.e. a spatial, model of the mind, in which three psychological systems are related spatially and which is organised into three psychical “localities” or systems, already familiar from The Interpretation of Dreams (Freud 1900a): system Perception-Consciousness (Cs.) or sometimes referred to just as system Perception (Pcpt.),2 system Preconscious (Pcs.) and system Unconscious (Ucs.).

CONTACT Patrizia Giampieri-Deutsch Patrizia.Giampieri-Deutsch@univie.ac.at

1It should be noted here that the following writings of Freud’s from the Revised Standard Edition [RSE] were kindly made available to me by the publishers of the RSE, Rowman and Littlefield, in advance of publication: The Ego and the Id (Freud 1923b, SE XIX, 12–66/RSE XIX, 10–58), including the abstract of the lecture “Some Remarks on the Unconscious” (Freud 1922f, SE XIX, 3–4/RSE XIX, 4) printed within the “Editors’ Introduction”, and Lecture XXXI “The Dissection of the Psychical Personality” from the New Introductory Lectures on Psychoanalysis (Freud 1933a [1932], SE XXII, 57–80/RSE XXII, 51–71). I am grateful to the publishers and the editor of the RSE, Mark Solms, for providing access to these documents. Where quotations are taken from the RSE, this is explicitly mentioned in brackets.

responseSet.title: What’s in a Word? A Brief Reflection on Why the Understanding of Freud is Not Changed by Replacing the Word “Instinct” with “Drive” and the Importance of Reading in Context
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What’s in a Word? A Brief Reflection on Why the Understanding of Freud is Not Changed by Replacing the Word “Instinct” with “Drive” and the Importance of Reading in Context

Rachel Blass

Rachel Blass

rachelblass@gmail.com

Rachel Blass  

Freud translation, instinct vs. drive, Klein

In this brief paper I argue that to understand Freud is to understand the meanings of his ideas. The choice of words to describe these ideas is usually relatively insignificant and does not in itself determine understanding. Thus, the translation of Trieb as “drive” rather than “instinct” does not change the phenomena that Freud addresses through the term Trieb, nor does it open us to the understanding of Freud’s profound, complex and evolving ideas regarding these phenomena. To think that it can, in fact, limits understanding. This runs counter to a popular view that Strachey’s translation of Trieb as “instinct” led the Anglo-American analytic community to see Freud as more biological and mechanistic than he actually was. Accepting the popular view would make the change of the term, which does occur in the Revised Standard Edition, seem especially significant. Evidence against this popular view may be seen in the fact that Klein and her followers, like Strachey himself, commonly use the term “instinct” and yet their understanding, which emerges from a close study of Freud in context and the phenomena to which he refers, maintains and develops the richness of Freud’s thinking and does not at all offer a biological or mechanistic perspective. Important to the understanding of Freud in context are his early letters to Fliess, and thus it is very unfortunate that the unabridged version of these letters which became available in 1985 were not included in the RSE.
